text.NRSV: The words of the Teacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: The words of the Proclaimer, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
text.KJV: The words of the Preacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem.
text.NABRE: The words of David’s son, Qoheleth, king in Jerusalem:
text.DRB: The words of Ecclesiastes, the son of David, king of Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Vanity of vanities, says the Teacher, vanity of vanities! All is vanity.
text.Rotherham: Vanity of vanities! saith the Proclaimer, vanity of vanities! all, is vanity.
text.KJV: Vanity of vanities, saith the Preacher, vanity of vanities; all is vanity.
text.NABRE: Vanity of vanities, says Qoheleth, vanity of vanities! All things are vanity!
text.DRB: Vanity of vanities, said Ecclesiastes: vanity of vanities, and all things are vanity.

text.NRSV: What do people gain from all the toil at which they toil under the sun?
text.Rotherham: What profit hath Man, in all his toil wherewith he toileth under the sun?
text.KJV: What profit hath a man of all his labour which he taketh under the sun?
text.NABRE: Vanity of Human Toil What profit have we from all the toil which we toil at under the sun?
text.DRB: What hath a man more of all his labour, whereby he laboureth under the sun?

text.NRSV: A generation goes, and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever.
text.Rotherham: Generation, goeth and, generation, cometh, but, the earth, unto times age-abiding, remaineth.
text.KJV: One generation passeth away, and another generation cometh: but the earth abideth for ever.
text.NABRE: One generation departs and another generation comes, but the world forever stays.
text.DRB: Generation passeth, and generation cometh: but the earth standeth for ever.

text.NRSV: The sun rises and the sun goes down, and hurries to the place where it rises.
text.Rotherham: And the sun, breaketh forth, and the sun, goeth in,—yea, unto his own place, he panteth, from whence he brake forth.
text.KJV: The sun also ariseth, and the sun goeth down, and hasteth to his place where he arose.
text.NABRE: The sun rises and the sun sets; then it presses on to the place where it rises.
text.DRB: The sun riseth, and goeth down, and returneth to his place: and there rising again,

text.NRSV: The wind blows to the south, and goes around to the north; round and round goes the wind, and on its circuits the wind returns.
text.Rotherham: Going unto the south, and circling unto the north,—circling, circling continually, is, the wind, and, over its own circuits, returneth the wind.
text.KJV: The wind goeth toward the south, and turneth about unto the north; it whirleth about continually, and the wind returneth again according to his circuits.
text.NABRE: Shifting south, then north, back and forth shifts the wind, constantly shifting its course.
text.DRB: compasseth by the South, and bendeth to the North: compassing all things, goeth forward in circuit, & returneth unto his circles.

text.NRSV: All streams run to the sea, but the sea is not full; to the place where the streams flow, there they continue to flow.
text.Rotherham: All the streams, flow into the sea, yet, the sea, is not full,—unto the place whither the streams flow, thither, do they again flow.
text.KJV: All the rivers run into the sea; yet the sea is not full; unto the place from whence the rivers come, thither they return again.
text.NABRE: All rivers flow to the sea, yet never does the sea become full. To the place where they flow, the rivers continue to flow.
text.DRB: All rivers enter into the sea, and the sea overfloweth not: to the place, whence the rivers issue forth, they do return, that they may flow again.

text.NRSV: All things are wearisome; more than one can express; the eye is not satisfied with seeing, or the ear filled with hearing.
text.Rotherham: All words, are weak, unable is any man to tell,—not satisfied is the eye by seeing, nor filled is the ear with hearing.
text.KJV: All things are full of labour; man cannot utter it: the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
text.NABRE: All things are wearisome, too wearisome for words. The eye is not satisfied by seeing nor has the ear enough of hearing.
text.DRB: All things are hard: man cannot explicate them in word. The eye is not filled with seeing, neither is the ear filled with hearing.

text.NRSV: What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done; there is nothing new under the sun.
text.Rotherham: That which hath been, is the same that shall be, and, that which hath been done, is the same that shall be done,—and there is, nothing new, under the sun.
text.KJV: The thing that hath been, it is that which shall be; and that which is done is that which shall be done: and there is no new thing under the sun.
text.NABRE: What has been, that will be; what has been done, that will be done. Nothing is new under the sun!
text.DRB: What is that hath been? The same thing that shall be. What is that hath been done? The same that is to be done.

text.NRSV: Is there a thing of which it is said, “See, this is new”? It has been already, in the ages before us.
text.Rotherham: Is there a thing, of which it can be said, See here, it is, new? Already, hath it been, for ages, [it is something] which was before us.
text.KJV: Is there any thing whereof it may be said, See, this is new? it hath been already of old time, which was before us.
text.NABRE: Even the thing of which we say, “See, this is new!” has already existed in the ages that preceded us.
text.DRB: Nothing under the sun is new, neither is any man able to say: Behold this is new: for it hath already gone before in the ages that were before us.

text.NRSV: The people of long ago are not remembered, nor will there be any remembrance of people yet to come by those who come after them.
text.Rotherham: There is, no remembrance, of the thing before,—nor, even of the things after, which shall be, will there be any remembrance, with them who shall come after.
text.KJV: There is no remembrance of former things; neither shall there be any remembrance of things that are to come with those that shall come after.
text.NABRE: There is no remembrance of past generations; nor will future generations be remembered by those who come after them.
text.DRB: There is no memory of former things: but neither of those things verily, which hereafter are to come, shall there be remembrance with them, that shall be in the later end.

text.NRSV: I, the Teacher, when king over Israel in Jerusalem,
text.Rotherham: I, the Proclaimer, was king over Israel, in Jerusalem.
text.KJV: I the Preacher was king over Israel in Jerusalem.
text.NABRE: I. Qoheleth’s Investigation of Life Twofold Introduction. I, Qoheleth, was king over Israel in Jerusalem,
text.DRB: I Ecclesiastes have been king of Israel in Jerusalem,

text.NRSV: applied my mind to seek and to search out by wisdom all that is done under heaven; it is an unhappy business that God has given to human beings to be busy with.
text.Rotherham: And I gave my heart to seek and to search out, wisely, concerning all things which are done under the heavens,—the same, is the vexatious employment God hath given to the sons of men, to work toilsomely therein,
text.KJV: And I gave my heart to seek and search out by wisdom concerning all things that are done under heaven: this sore travail hath God given to the sons of man to be exercised therewith.
text.NABRE: and I applied my mind to search and investigate in wisdom all things that are done under the sun. A bad business God has given to human beings to be busied with.
text.DRB: and have proposed in my mind to seek and search wisely of all things, that are done under the sun. This very evil occupation hath God given to the children of men, that they might be occupied in it.

text.NRSV: I saw all the deeds that are done under the sun; and see, all is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: I saw all the works which were done under the sun,—and lo! all, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: I have seen all the works that are done under the sun; and, behold, all is vanity and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: I have seen all things that are done under the sun, and behold, all is vanity and a chase after wind.
text.DRB: I have seen all things, that are done under the sun, and behold all are vanity, & affliction of spirit.

text.NRSV: What is crooked cannot be made straight, and what is lacking cannot be numbered.
text.Rotherham: That which is crooked, cannot be straight,—and, that which is wanting, cannot be reckoned.
text.KJV: That which is crooked cannot be made straight: and that which is wanting cannot be numbered.
text.NABRE: What is crooked cannot be made straight, and you cannot count what is not there.
text.DRB: The perverse are hardly corrected, and the number of fools is infinite.

text.NRSV: I said to myself, “I have acquired great wisdom, surpassing all who were over Jerusalem before me; and my mind has had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.”
text.Rotherham: Spake, I, in my heart, saying, As for me, lo! I have become great, and have gathered wisdom, beyond any one who hath been before me over Jerusalem,—and, my heart, hath seen much wisdom and knowledge:
text.KJV: I communed with mine own heart, saying, Lo, I am come to great estate, and have gotten more wisdom than all they that have been before me in Jerusalem: yea, my heart had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.
text.NABRE: Though I said to myself, “See, I have greatly increased my wisdom beyond all who were before me in Jerusalem, and my mind has broad experience of wisdom and knowledge,”
text.DRB: I have spoken in my heart, saying: Lo I have been made great, and have gone beyond all in wisdom, that were before me in Jerusalem: and my mind hath contemplated many things wisely, and I have learned.

text.NRSV: And I applied my mind to know wisdom and to know madness and folly. I perceived that this also is but a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: yea I have given my heart, to know wisdom, and to know madness, and folly,—I know that, even this, is a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: And I gave my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly: I perceived that this also is vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: yet when I applied my mind to know wisdom and knowledge, madness and folly, I learned that this also is a chase after wind.
text.DRB: And I have given my heart to know prudence, and doctrine, and errors, and folly: and I have perceived that in these also there was labour, and affliction of spirit,

text.NRSV: For in much wisdom is much vexation, and those who increase knowledge increase sorrow.
text.Rotherham: For, in much wisdom, is much vexation,—and, he that increaseth knowledge, increaseth pain.
text.KJV: For in much wisdom is much grief: and he that increaseth knowledge increaseth sorrow.
text.NABRE: For in much wisdom there is much sorrow; whoever increases knowledge increases grief.
text.DRB: for that in much wisdom there is much indignation: and he that addeth knowledge, addeth also labour.

text.NRSV: I said to myself, “Come now, I will make a test of pleasure; enjoy yourself.” But again, this also was vanity.
text.Rotherham: Said, I, in my heart, Come now! I will prove thee with gladness, and look thou on blessedness,—but lo! even that, was vanity.
text.KJV: I said in mine heart, Go to now, I will prove thee with mirth, therefore enjoy pleasure: and, behold, this also is vanity.
text.NABRE: I said in my heart, “Come, now, let me try you with pleasure and the enjoyment of good things.” See, this too was vanity.
text.DRB: Isaid therefore in my heart: I will go, & flow in delights, and enjoy good things. And I saw that this also was vanity.

text.NRSV: I said of laughter, “It is mad,” and of pleasure, “What use is it?”
text.Rotherham: Of laughter, I said, Madness! and, of mirth, What can it do?
text.KJV: I said of laughter, It is mad: and of mirth, What doeth it?
text.NABRE: Of laughter I said: “Mad!” and of mirth: “What good does this do?”
text.DRB: Laughter I have reputed error: and to joy I have said: Why art thou deceived in vain?

text.NRSV: I searched with my mind how to cheer my body with wine—my mind still guiding me with wisdom—and how to lay hold on folly, until I might see what was good for mortals to do under heaven during the few days of their life.
text.Rotherham: I sought out with my heart, to cherish with wine, my flesh,—but, my heart, was to guide with wisdom, even in laying hold of folly, until I should see which was blessedness for the sons of men, as to that which they could do, under the heavens, during the number of the days of their life.
text.KJV: I sought in mine heart to give myself unto wine, yet acquainting mine heart with wisdom; and to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was that good for the sons of men, which they should do under the heaven all the days of their life.
text.NABRE: Guided by wisdom, I probed with my mind how to beguile my senses with wine and take up folly, until I should understand what is good for human beings to do under the heavens during the limited days of their lives.
text.DRB: I have thought in my heart to withdraw my flesh from wine, that I might transfer my mind to wisdom, and might avoid folly, till I might see what should be profitable for the children of men: what is needful to be done under the sun, in the number of the days of their life.

text.NRSV: I made great works; I built houses and planted vineyards for myself;
text.Rotherham: I enlarged my works,—I built me houses, I planted me vineyards;
text.KJV: I made me great works; I builded me houses; I planted me vineyards:
text.NABRE: I undertook great works; I built myself houses and planted vineyards;
text.DRB: I have magnified my works, I have built me houses, & planted vineyards,

text.NRSV: I made myself gardens and parks, and planted in them all kinds of fruit trees.
text.Rotherham: I made me gardens, and parks,—I planted in them trees of every kind of fruit;
text.KJV: I made me gardens and orchards, and I planted trees in them of all kind of fruits:
text.NABRE: I made gardens and parks, and in them set out fruit trees of all sorts.
text.DRB: I have made gardens, and orchards, and set them with trees of all kinds,

text.NRSV: I made myself pools from which to water the forest of growing trees.
text.Rotherham: I made me pools of water,—to irrigate therefrom the thick-set saplings growing up into trees:
text.KJV: I made me pools of water, to water therewith the wood that bringeth forth trees:
text.NABRE: And I constructed for myself reservoirs to water a flourishing woodland.
text.DRB: and I have made me ponds of waters, to water the wood of springing trees,

text.NRSV: I bought male and female slaves, and had slaves who were born in my house; I also had great possessions of herds and flocks, more than any who had been before me in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: I acquired, men-servants and women-servants, and, the children of the household, were mine,—also possessions, herds and flocks in abundance, were mine, beyond all who had been before me in Jerusalem;
text.KJV: I got me servants and maidens, and had servants born in my house; also I had great possessions of great and small cattle above all that were in Jerusalem before me:
text.NABRE: I acquired male and female slaves, and had slaves who were born in my house. I also owned vast herds of cattle and flocks of sheep, more than all who had been before me in Jerusalem.
text.DRB: I have possessed men-servants and women-servants, and have had a great family: herds also, and great flocks of sheep, above all that were before me in Jerusalem:

text.NRSV: I also gathered for myself silver and gold and the treasure of kings and of the provinces; I got singers, both men and women, and delights of the flesh, and many concubines.
text.Rotherham: I heaped me up, both silver and gold, and the peculiar treasure of kings, and provinces,—I provided me singing-men and singing-women, and the delights of the sons of men, a wife and wives.
text.KJV: I gathered me also silver and gold, and the peculiar treasure of kings and of the provinces: I gat me men singers and women singers, and the delights of the sons of men, as musical instruments, and that of all sorts.
text.NABRE: I amassed for myself silver and gold, and the treasures of kings and provinces. I provided for myself male and female singers and delights of men, many women.
text.DRB: I have heaped together to myself silver, and gold, and the substance of kings, and provinces: I made me singing men, & singing women, and the delights of the children of men: cups, and goblets to serve to pour out wines:

text.NRSV: So I became great and surpassed all who were before me in Jerusalem; also my wisdom remained with me.
text.Rotherham: So I became great, and increased, more than any one who had been before me in Jerusalem,—moreover, my wisdom, remained with me;
text.KJV: So I was great, and increased more than all that were before me in Jerusalem: also my wisdom remained with me.
text.NABRE: I accumulated much more than all others before me in Jerusalem; my wisdom, too, stayed with me.
text.DRB: and I surpassed in riches all that were before me in Jerusalem: wisdom also hath persevered with me.

text.NRSV: Whatever my eyes desired I did not keep from them; I kept my heart from no pleasure, for my heart found pleasure in all my toil, and this was my reward for all my toil.
text.Rotherham: and, nothing that mine eyes asked, withheld I from them,—I did not keep back my heart from any gladness, for, my heart, obtained gladness out of all my toil, and so, this, was my portion, out of all my toil.
text.KJV: And whatsoever mine eyes desired I kept not from them, I withheld not my heart from any joy; for my heart rejoiced in all my labour: and this was my portion of all my labour.
text.NABRE: Nothing that my eyes desired did I deny them, nor did I deprive myself of any joy; rather, my heart rejoiced in the fruit of all my toil. This was my share for all my toil.
text.DRB: And all things that mine eyes desired, I have not denied to them: neither have I stayed my heart, but that it enjoyed all pleasure, & delighted it-self in these things, which I had prepared: and this I esteemed my portion, if I did use my labour.

text.NRSV: Then I considered all that my hands had done and the toil I had spent in doing it, and again, all was vanity and a chasing after wind, and there was nothing to be gained under the sun.
text.Rotherham: When, I, looked upon all my works, which my hands had made, and on my toil, whereon I had toilsomely wrought, then lo! all, was vanity, and feeding on wind, and there was no profit under the sun.
text.KJV: Then I looked on all the works that my hands had wrought, and on the labour that I had laboured to do: and, behold, all was vanity and vexation of spirit, and there was no profit under the sun.
text.NABRE: But when I turned to all the works that my hands had wrought, and to the fruit of the toil for which I had toiled so much, see! all was vanity and a chase after wind. There is no profit under the sun.
text.DRB: And when I had turned myself to all the works which my hands had done, & to the labours wherein I had sweat in vain, I saw in all things vanity, and affliction of mind, & nothing to be permanent under the sun.

text.NRSV: So I turned to consider wisdom and madness and folly; for what can the one do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.
text.Rotherham: Thus turned, I, to look at wisdom, and madness and folly,—for what can the man [do more] who cometh after the king? [save] that which, already, men have done.
text.KJV: And I turned myself to behold wisdom, and madness, and folly: for what can the man do that cometh after the king? even that which hath been already done.
text.NABRE: What about one who succeeds a king? He can do only what has already been done. Study of Wisdom and Folly. I went on to the consideration of wisdom, madness and folly.
text.DRB: I passed further to contemplate wisdom, and errors, and folly (what is man, quoth I, that he can follow the King his Maker?)

text.NRSV: Then I saw that wisdom excels folly as light excels darkness.
text.Rotherham: Then saw, I, that wisdom doth excel folly,—as far as light excelleth darkness.
text.KJV: Then I saw that wisdom excelleth folly, as far as light excelleth darkness.
text.NABRE: And I saw that wisdom has as much profit over folly as light has over darkness.
text.DRB: and I saw that wisdom so much excelled folly, as light differeth from darkness.

text.NRSV: The wise have eyes in their head, but fools walk in darkness. Yet I perceived that the same fate befalls all of them.
text.Rotherham: As for the wise man, his eyes, are in his head, whereas, the dullard, in darkness, doth walk,—but, I myself, knew that, one destiny, happeneth to them, all.
text.KJV: The wise man’s eyes are in his head; but the fool walketh in darkness: and I myself perceived also that one event happeneth to them all.
text.NABRE: Wise people have eyes in their heads, but fools walk in darkness. Yet I knew that the same lot befalls both.
text.DRB: The eyes of a wiseman are in his head: the fool walketh in darkness: and I have learned that there was one death of both.

text.NRSV: Then I said to myself, “What happens to the fool will happen to me also; why then have I been so very wise?” And I said to myself that this also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: Then said, I, in my heart, As it happeneth to the dullard, even to me, will it happen, but wherefore, then, became, I, wise to excess? Therefore spake I, in my heart, Even this, is vanity.
text.KJV: Then said I in my heart, As it happeneth to the fool, so it happeneth even to me; and why was I then more wise? Then I said in my heart, that this also is vanity.
text.NABRE: So I said in my heart, if the fool’s lot is to befall me also, why should I be wise? Where is the profit? And in my heart I decided that this too is vanity.
text.DRB: And I said in my heart: if the fall of the fool & mine shall be one, what doth it profit me, that I have bestowed greater labour for wisdom? And speaking with my mind, I perceived that this also was vanity.

text.NRSV: For there is no enduring remembrance of the wise or of fools, seeing that in the days to come all will have been long forgotten. How can the wise die just like fools?
text.Rotherham: For there is no remembrance of a wise man, more than of a dullard, unto times age-abiding,—seeing that, already, in the days to come, all hath been forgotten, how then cometh it that the wise man dieth equally with the dullard?
text.KJV: For there is no remembrance of the wise more than of the fool for ever; seeing that which now is in the days to come shall all be forgotten. And how dieth the wise man? as the fool.
text.NABRE: The wise person will have no more abiding remembrance than the fool; for in days to come both will have been forgotten. How is it that the wise person dies like the fool!
text.DRB: For there shall be no memory of the wise in like manner as of the fool for ever, and the times to come shall cover all things together with oblivion: the learned dieth in like manner as the unlearned.

text.NRSV: So I hated life, because what is done under the sun was grievous to me; for all is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Therefore I hated life, for, a vexation unto me, was the work which was done under the sun,—for, all, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: Therefore I hated life; because the work that is wrought under the sun is grievous unto me: for all is vanity and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: Therefore I detested life, since for me the work that is done under the sun is bad; for all is vanity and a chase after wind.
text.DRB: And therefore I have been weary of my life, seeing all things under the sun to be evil, and all things vanity and affliction of spirit.

text.NRSV: I hated all my toil in which I had toiled under the sun, seeing that I must leave it to those who come after me
text.Rotherham: Therefore hated, I, all my toil, wherein I was toiling, under the sun,—in that I should leave it for the man who should come after me;
text.KJV: Yea, I hated all my labour which I had taken under the sun: because I should leave it unto the man that shall be after me.
text.NABRE: Study of the Fruits of Toil To Others the Profits. And I detested all the fruits of my toil under the sun, because I must leave them to the one who is to come after me.
text.DRB: Again, I detested all mine industry, wherewith I have laboured under the sun most studiously, being like to have an heir after me:

text.NRSV: —and who knows whether they will be wise or foolish? Yet they will be master of all for which I toiled and used my wisdom under the sun. This also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: and who could know whether a, wise man, he would be or a foolish, and yet he would lord it over all my toil, wherein I had toiled and wherein I had acted wisely, under the sun,—even this, was vanity.
text.KJV: And who knoweth whether he shall be a wise man or a fool? yet shall he have rule over all my labour wherein I have laboured, and wherein I have shewed myself wise under the sun. This is also vanity.
text.NABRE: And who knows whether that one will be wise or a fool? Yet that one will take control of all the fruits of my toil and wisdom under the sun. This also is vanity.
text.DRB: whom I know not, whether he will be a wiseman or a fool, and he shall rule in my labours, wherewith I have sweat and have been careful: and is there any thing so vain?

text.NRSV: So I turned and gave my heart up to despair concerning all the toil of my labors under the sun,
text.Rotherham: Then resolved I, to give my heart over to despair,—concerning all the toil, wherein I had toiled, under the sun.
text.KJV: Therefore I went about to cause my heart to despair of all the labour which I took under the sun.
text.NABRE: So my heart turned to despair over all the fruits of my toil under the sun.
text.DRB: Wherefore I ceased, and my heart hath renounced to labour any more under the sun.

text.NRSV: because sometimes one who has toiled with wisdom and knowledge and skill must leave all to be enjoyed by another who did not toil for it. This also is vanity and a great evil.
text.Rotherham: For here is a man, whose toil hath been with wisdom and with knowledge and with skill,—yet, to a man who hath not toiled therein, shall he leave it as his portion, even this, was vanity and a great vexation.
text.KJV: For there is a man whose labour is in wisdom, and in knowledge, and in equity; yet to a man that hath not laboured therein shall he leave it for his portion. This also is vanity and a great evil.
text.NABRE: For here is one who has toiled with wisdom and knowledge and skill, and that one’s legacy must be left to another who has not toiled for it. This also is vanity and a great evil.
text.DRB: For whereas one laboureth in wisdom, and doctrine, and carefulness, he leaveth the things gotten to an idle man: and this therefore is vanity, and great evil.

text.NRSV: What do mortals get from all the toil and strain they endure under the sun?
text.Rotherham: For what hath the man for all his toil, and for the striving of his heart,—wherein, he himself, toiled under the sun?
text.KJV: For what hath man of all his labour, and of the vexation of his heart, wherein he hath laboured under the sun?
text.NABRE: For what profit comes to mortals from all the toil and anxiety of heart with which they toil under the sun?
text.DRB: For what profit shall be to a man of all his labour, and affliction of spirit, wherewith he is vexed under the sun?

text.NRSV: For all their days are full of pain, and their work is a vexation; even at night their minds do not rest. This also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: For, all his days, are pains, and, vexatious, is his employment, even in the night, his heart lieth not down,—even this, was, vanity.
text.KJV: For all his days are sorrows, and his travail grief; yea, his heart taketh not rest in the night. This is also vanity.
text.NABRE: Every day sorrow and grief are their occupation; even at night their hearts are not at rest. This also is vanity.
text.DRB: All his days are full of sorrows and miseries, neither by night doth he rest in mind: and is not this vanity?

text.NRSV: There is nothing better for mortals than to eat and drink, and find enjoyment in their toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God;
text.Rotherham: There was nothing more blessed for Man [than] that he should eat and drink, and see his desireth for blessedness in his toil,—even this, saw, I myself, that, from the hand of God, it was.
text.KJV: There is nothing better for a man, than that he should eat and drink, and that he should make his soul enjoy good in his labour. This also I saw, that it was from the hand of God.
text.NABRE: There is nothing better for mortals than to eat and drink and provide themselves with good things from their toil. Even this, I saw, is from the hand of God.
text.DRB: Is it not better to eat and drink, and shew unto his soul good things of his labours? & this is of the hand of God.

text.NRSV: for apart from him who can eat or who can have enjoyment?
text.Rotherham: For who could eat and who could enjoy, so well as I?
text.KJV: For who can eat, or who else can hasten hereunto, more than I?
text.NABRE: For who can eat or drink apart from God?
text.DRB: Who shall so devour, and flow with delights as I?

text.NRSV: For to the one who pleases him God gives wisdom and knowledge and joy; but to the sinner he gives the work of gathering and heaping, only to give to one who pleases God. This also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: For, to a man who is good before him, hath he given wisdom and knowledge and gladness,—whereas, to the sinner, he hath given employment, to gather and heap up, to give to one who is good before God, even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: For God giveth to a man that is good in his sight wisdom, and knowledge, and joy: but to the sinner he giveth travail, to gather and to heap up, that he may give to him that is good before God. This also is vanity and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: For to the one who pleases God, he gives wisdom and knowledge and joy; but to the one who displeases, God gives the task of gathering possessions for the one who pleases God. This also is vanity and a chase after wind.
text.DRB: To a man good in his sight, God hath given wisdom, and knowledge, and joy: but to the sinner he hath given affliction, and superfluous care, to add, and to gather together, and deliver it to him that hath pleased God: but this also is vanity, & vain carefulness of the mind.

text.NRSV: For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven:
text.Rotherham: For, every thing, there is a season,—and a time for every pursuit, under the heavens:—
text.KJV: To every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven:
text.NABRE: Chapter 3 No One Can Determine the Right Time to Act There is an appointed time for everything, and a time for every affair under the heavens.
text.DRB: All things have a time, and in their spaces all things pass under heaven.

text.NRSV: a time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up what is planted;
text.Rotherham: A time to be born, and a time to die,—A time to plant, and a time to uproot what is planted;
text.KJV: A time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up that which is planted;
text.NABRE: A time to give birth, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to uproot the plant.
text.DRB: Atime to be born, & a time to die. A time to plant, & a time to pluck up that which was planted.

text.NRSV: a time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up;
text.Rotherham: A time to kill, and a time to heal,—A time to break down, and a time to build up;
text.KJV: A time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up;
text.NABRE: A time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to tear down, and a time to build.
text.DRB: Atime to kill, and a time to heal. A time to destroy, and a time to build.

text.NRSV: a time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance;
text.Rotherham: A time to weep, and a time to laugh,—A time to wail, and a time to dance for joy;
text.KJV: A time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance;
text.NABRE: A time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance.
text.DRB: Atime to weep, & a time to laugh. A time to mourn, and a time to dance.

text.NRSV: a time to throw away stones, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing;
text.Rotherham: A time to cast away stones, and a time to heap up stones,—A time to embrace, and a time to be far from loving embrace;
text.KJV: A time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing;
text.NABRE: A time to scatter stones, and a time to gather them; a time to embrace, and a time to be far from embraces.
text.DRB: Atime to disperse stones, and a time to gather. A time to embrace, and a time to be far from embracings.

text.NRSV: a time to seek, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to throw away;
text.Rotherham: A time to seek, and a time to give up as lost,—A time to keep, and a time to cast away;
text.KJV: A time to get, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away;
text.NABRE: A time to seek, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away.
text.DRB: Atime to get, and a time to lose. A time to keep, and a time to cast away.

text.NRSV: a time to tear, and a time to sew; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak;
text.Rotherham: A time to rend, and a time to sew,—A time to be silent, and a time to speak;
text.KJV: A time to rend, and a time to sew; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak;
text.NABRE: A time to rend, and a time to sew; a time to be silent, and a time to speak.
text.DRB: Atime to rent, and a time to sow together. A time to keep silence, & a time to speak.

text.NRSV: a time for love, and a time for hate; a time for war, and a time for peace.
text.Rotherham: A time to love and a time to hate,—A time of war, and a time of peace.
text.KJV: A time to love, and a time to hate; a time of war, and a time of peace.
text.NABRE: A time to love, and a time to hate; a time of war, and a time of peace.
text.DRB: Atime to love, and a time of hatred. A time of war, and a time of peace.

text.NRSV: What gain have the workers from their toil?
text.Rotherham: What profit hath he that worketh, in that wherein, himself, hath toiled?
text.KJV: What profit hath he that worketh in that wherein he laboureth?
text.NABRE: What profit have workers from their toil?
text.DRB: What hath man more of his labour?

text.NRSV: I have seen the business that God has given to everyone to be busy with.
text.Rotherham: I looked at the employment which God hath given to the sons of men, to work therein:
text.KJV: I have seen the travail, which God hath given to the sons of men to be exercised in it.
text.NABRE: I have seen the business that God has given to mortals to be busied about.
text.DRB: I have seen the affliction, which God hath given to the children of men, that they may be distracted in it.

text.NRSV: He has made everything suitable for its time; moreover he has put a sense of past and future into their minds, yet they cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end.
text.Rotherham: Everything, hath he made beautiful in its own time,—also, intelligence, hath he put in their heart, without which men could not find out the work which God hath wrought, from the beginning even unto the end.
text.KJV: He hath made every thing beautiful in his time: also he hath set the world in their heart, so that no man can find out the work that God maketh from the beginning to the end.
text.NABRE: God has made everything appropriate to its time, but has put the timeless into their hearts so they cannot find out, from beginning to end, the work which God has done.
text.DRB: He hath made all things good in their time, and hath delivered the world to their disputation, and that man cannot find the work, which God hath wrought from the beginning unto the end.

text.NRSV: I know that there is nothing better for them than to be happy and enjoy themselves as long as they live;
text.Rotherham: I know that there is no blessedness in them,—save to be glad, and to do well with one’s life.
text.KJV: I know that there is no good in them, but for a man to rejoice, and to do good in his life.
text.NABRE: I recognized that there is nothing better than to rejoice and to do well during life.
text.DRB: And I have known that there was no better thing than to rejoice, and to do well in his life.

text.NRSV: moreover, it is God’s gift that all should eat and drink and take pleasure in all their toil.
text.Rotherham: Though indeed, that any man should eat and drink, and see blessedness, in all his toil, it is, the gift of God.
text.KJV: And also that every man should eat and drink, and enjoy the good of all his labour, it is the gift of God.
text.NABRE: Moreover, that all can eat and drink and enjoy the good of all their toil—this is a gift of God.
text.DRB: For every man, that eateth and drinketh, and seeth good of his labour, this is the gift of God.

text.NRSV: I know that whatever God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it, nor anything taken from it; God has done this, so that all should stand in awe before him.
text.Rotherham: I know, that, whatsoever God doeth, the same, shall be age-abiding, unto it, there is nothing to add, and, from it, there is nothing to take away,—and, God, hath done it, that men should stand in awe before him.
text.KJV: I know that, whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever: nothing can be put to it, nor any thing taken from it: and God doeth it, that men should fear before him.
text.NABRE: I recognized that whatever God does will endure forever; there is no adding to it, or taking from it. Thus has God done that he may be revered.
text.DRB: I have learned that all the works, which God hath made, persevere for ever: we cannot add any thing, nor take away from those things, which God hath made that he may be feared.

text.NRSV: That which is, already has been; that which is to be, already is; and God seeks out what has gone by.
text.Rotherham: That which was, already, had been, and, that which shall be, already, shall have been,—but, God, seeketh that which hath been chased away.
text.KJV: That which hath been is now; and that which is to be hath already been; and God requireth that which is past.
text.NABRE: What now is has already been; what is to be, already is: God retrieves what has gone by.
text.DRB: That which hath been made, the same is permanent: the things that shall be, have already been: and God restoreth that which is past.

text.NRSV: Moreover I saw under the sun that in the place of justice, wickedness was there, and in the place of righteousness, wickedness was there as well.
text.Rotherham: Then, again, I saw under the sun, the place of justice, that there was lawlessness, and, the place of righteousness, that there was lawlessness.
text.KJV: And moreover I saw under the sun the place of judgment, that wickedness was there; and the place of righteousness, that iniquity was there.
text.NABRE: The Problem of Retribution. And still under the sun in the judgment place I saw wickedness, and wickedness also in the seat of justice.
text.DRB: Isaw under the sun in the place of judgement impiety, and in the place of justice iniquity.

text.NRSV: I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for he has appointed a time for every matter, and for every work.
text.Rotherham: Said, I, in my heart, Both the righteous and the lawless, will God judge,—for [there will be] a time for every pursuit, and concerning every work—there.
text.KJV: I said in mine heart, God shall judge the righteous and the wicked: for there is a time there for every purpose and for every work.
text.NABRE: I said in my heart, both the just and the wicked God will judge, since a time is set for every affair and for every work.
text.DRB: And I said in my heart: the just and the impious God will judge, and then shall be the time of every thing.

text.NRSV: I said in my heart with regard to human beings that God is testing them to show that they are but animals.
text.Rotherham: Said, I, in my heart, as concerning the sons of men, That God was minded to prove them,—and that they might see, that they were beasts, of themselves.
text.KJV: I said in mine heart concerning the estate of the sons of men, that God might manifest them, and that they might see that they themselves are beasts.
text.NABRE: I said in my heart: As for human beings, it is God’s way of testing them and of showing that they are in themselves like beasts.
text.DRB: Isaid in my heart of the children of men, that God would prove them, and shew them to be like beasts.

text.NRSV: For the fate of humans and the fate of animals is the same; as one dies, so dies the other. They all have the same breath, and humans have no advantage over the animals; for all is vanity.
text.Rotherham: For, as regardeth the destiny of the sons of men and the destiny of beasts, one fate, have they, as dieth the one, so, dieth the other, and, one spirit, have they all,—and, the pre-eminence of man over beast, is nothing, for, all, were vanity:
text.KJV: For that which befalleth the sons of men befalleth beasts; even one thing befalleth them: as the one dieth, so dieth the other; yea, they have all one breath; so that a man hath no preeminence above a beast: for all is vanity.
text.NABRE: For the lot of mortals and the lot of beasts is the same lot: The one dies as well as the other. Both have the same life breath. Human beings have no advantage over beasts, but all is vanity.
text.DRB: Therefore there is one death of man, and beasts, and the condition of both equal: as man dieth, so they also die: all things breathe alike, and man hath nothing more than beast: all things are subject to vanity,

text.NRSV: All go to one place; all are from the dust, and all turn to dust again.
text.Rotherham: all, go unto one place,—all, came from the dust, and all, return to the dust.
text.KJV: All go unto one place; all are of the dust, and all turn to dust again.
text.NABRE: Both go to the same place; both were made from the dust, and to the dust they both return.
text.DRB: and all things pass to one place: of earth they were made, and into earth they return together.

text.NRSV: Who knows whether the human spirit goes upward and the spirit of animals goes downward to the earth?
text.Rotherham: Who knoweth the spirit of the sons of men, whether it, ascendeth, above,—or the spirit of the beast, whether it, descendeth, below, to the earth?
text.KJV: Who knoweth the spirit of man that goeth upward, and the spirit of the beast that goeth downward to the earth?
text.NABRE: Who knows if the life breath of mortals goes upward and the life breath of beasts goes earthward?
text.DRB: Who knoweth if the spirit of the children of Adam ascend upward, and if the spirit of beasts descend downward?

text.NRSV: So I saw that there is nothing better than that all should enjoy their work, for that is their lot; who can bring them to see what will be after them?
text.Rotherham: So I saw, that there was nothing better than that a man should be glad in his works, for, that, is his portion,—for who can bring him in, to look upon that which shall be after him?
text.KJV: Wherefore I perceive that there is nothing better, than that a man should rejoice in his own works; for that is his portion: for who shall bring him to see what shall be after him?
text.NABRE: And I saw that there is nothing better for mortals than to rejoice in their work; for this is their lot. Who will let them see what is to come after them?
text.DRB: And I have found that nothing is better than for a man to rejoice in his work, and that this is his portion. For who shall bring him to know the things that shall be after him?

text.NRSV: Again I saw all the oppressions that are practiced under the sun. Look, the tears of the oppressed—with no one to comfort them! On the side of their oppressors there was power—with no one to comfort them.
text.Rotherham: Then again, I, considered all the oppressive deeds which were done under the sun,—and lo! the tears of the oppressed, and they have no comforter, and, on the side of their oppressing, is power, and they have no comforter.
text.KJV: So I returned, and considered all the oppressions that are done under the sun: and behold the tears of such as were oppressed, and they had no comforter; and on the side of their oppressors there was power; but they had no comforter.
text.NABRE: Again I saw all the oppressions that take place under the sun: the tears of the victims with none to comfort them! From the hand of their oppressors comes violence, and there is none to comfort them!
text.DRB: Iturned myself to other things, and I saw the oppressions, that are done under the sun, and the tears of the innocents, and no comforter: & that they cannot resist their violence, being destitute of all men's help.

text.NRSV: And I thought the dead, who have already died, more fortunate than the living, who are still alive;
text.Rotherham: So, I, pronounced happy the dead, who were, already, dead,—more than the living, who were living, still;
text.KJV: Wherefore I praised the dead which are already dead more than the living which are yet alive.
text.NABRE: And those now dead, I declared more fortunate in death than are the living to be still alive.
text.DRB: And I praised rather the dead, than the living:

text.NRSV: but better than both is the one who has not yet been, and has not seen the evil deeds that are done under the sun.
text.Rotherham: and, as better than both, him who had not yet come into being,—who had not seen the vexatious work, which was done under the sun.
text.KJV: Yea, better is he than both they, which hath not yet been, who hath not seen the evil work that is done under the sun.
text.NABRE: And better off than both is the yet unborn, who has not seen the wicked work that is done under the sun.
text.DRB: and happier than both have I judged him that is not yet born, nor hath seen the evils that are done under the sun.

text.NRSV: Then I saw that all toil and all skill in work come from one person’s envy of another. This also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Then saw, I, all the toil and all the skill of the work, that, for this, a man was envied of his neighbour,—even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: Again, I considered all travail, and every right work, that for this a man is envied of his neighbour. This is also vanity and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: Then I saw that all toil and skillful work is the rivalry of one person with another. This also is vanity and a chase after wind.
text.DRB: Again I have contemplated all the labours of men, and their industries I have perceived to lie open to the envy of their neighbour: and in this therefore there is vanity, and superfluous care.

text.NRSV: Fools fold their hands and consume their own flesh.
text.Rotherham: The dullard, claspeth his hands, and consumeth his own flesh.
text.KJV: The fool foldeth his hands together, and eateth his own flesh.
text.NABRE: “Fools fold their arms and consume their own flesh”—
text.DRB: Afool foldeth his hands together, and eateth his own flesh, saying:

text.NRSV: Better is a handful with quiet than two handfuls with toil, and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Better a handful—with quietness,—than both hands, full—with toil, and feeding on wind.
text.KJV: Better is an handful with quietness, than both the hands full with travail and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: Better is one handful with tranquility than two with toil and a chase after wind!
text.DRB: Better is an handful with rest, than both hands full with labour and affliction of mind.

text.NRSV: Again, I saw vanity under the sun:
text.Rotherham: Then again, I, looked at a vain thing under the sun:—
text.KJV: Then I returned, and I saw vanity under the sun.
text.NABRE: Companions and Successors. Again I saw this vanity under the sun:
text.DRB: Considering I found also another vanity under the sun:

text.NRSV: the case of solitary individuals, without companion or child or brother, yet there is no end to all their toil, and their eyes are never satisfied with riches. “For whom am I toiling,” they ask, “and depriving myself of pleasure?” This also is vanity and an unhappy business.
text.Rotherham: Here is one, without a second, even son or brother, he hath none, yet is there no end to all his toil, even his eye, is not satisfied with riches,—neither [saith he] For whom, am I toiling, and letting my soul want good? Even this, was vanity, yea a vexatious employment, it was!
text.KJV: There is one alone, and there is not a second; yea, he hath neither child nor brother: yet is there no end of all his labour; neither is his eye satisfied with riches; neither saith he, For whom do I labour, and bereave my soul of good? This is also vanity, yea, it is a sore travail.
text.NABRE: those all alone with no companion, with neither child nor sibling—with no end to all their toil, and no satisfaction from riches. For whom do I toil and deprive myself of good things? This also is vanity and a bad business.
text.DRB: There is one, and he hath not a second, not a son, not a brother, and yet he ceaseth not to labour, neither are his eyes satisfied with riches, neither doth he recount, saying: For whom do I labour, and defraud my soul of good things? In this also is vanity, and very ill affliction.

text.NRSV: Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.
text.Rotherham: Better are two, than one,—in that they have a good reward for their toil.
text.KJV: Two are better than one; because they have a good reward for their labour.
text.NABRE: Two are better than one: They get a good wage for their toil.
text.DRB: It is better therefore that two be together, than one: for they have profit of their society:

text.NRSV: For if they fall, one will lift up the other; but woe to one who is alone and falls and does not have another to help.
text.Rotherham: For, if the one should fall, [the other] would raise up his companion,—but alas! for him who is alone when he falleth, with no second to raise him up!
text.KJV: For if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow: but woe to him that is alone when he falleth; for he hath not another to help him up.
text.NABRE: If the one falls, the other will help the fallen one. But woe to the solitary person! If that one should fall, there is no other to help.
text.DRB: if one fall, he shall be stayed up of the other. Woe to him that is alone: because when he falleth, he hath none to lift him up.

text.NRSV: Again, if two lie together, they keep warm; but how can one keep warm alone?
text.Rotherham: Moreover, if two lie together, then have they warmth,—but how can, one, have warmth?
text.KJV: Again, if two lie together, then they have heat: but how can one be warm alone?
text.NABRE: So also, if two sleep together, they keep each other warm. How can one alone keep warm?
text.DRB: And if two sleep together, they shall warm each other: one how shall he be warmed?

text.NRSV: And though one might prevail against another, two will withstand one. A threefold cord is not quickly broken.
text.Rotherham: And, though an enemy should prevail against one, two, might make a stand before him,—and, a threefold cord, cannot soon be broken.
text.KJV: And if one prevail against him, two shall withstand him; and a threefold cord is not quickly broken.
text.NABRE: Where one alone may be overcome, two together can resist. A three-ply cord is not easily broken.
text.DRB: And if a man prevail against one, two resist him: a triple cord is hardly broken.

text.NRSV: Better is a poor but wise youth than an old but foolish king, who will no longer take advice.
text.Rotherham: Better a boy poor and wise,—than a king, old and stupid, who knoweth not how to take warning any longer.
text.KJV: Better is a poor and a wise child than an old and foolish king, who will no more be admonished.
text.NABRE: Better is a poor but wise youth than an old but foolish king who no longer knows caution;
text.DRB: Better is a child that is poor and wise, than a king old and foolish, that knoweth not to foresee for hereafter.

text.NRSV: One can indeed come out of prison to reign, even though born poor in the kingdom.
text.Rotherham: For, out of prison, came he forth to reign, yea, even in his own kingdom, was he born poor.
text.KJV: For out of prison he cometh to reign; whereas also he that is born in his kingdom becometh poor.
text.NABRE: for from a prison house he came forth to reign; despite his kingship he was born poor.
text.DRB: Because out of the prison and chains sometime there cometh one forth to a Kingdom: and another born in his Kingdom, is consumed with poverty.

text.NRSV: I saw all the living who, moving about under the sun, follow that youth, the king’s successor;
text.Rotherham: I saw all the living, who were going hither and thither under the sun,—[that they were] with the boy who was to be the second, who was to stand in the other’s place:—
text.KJV: I considered all the living which walk under the sun, with the second child that shall stand up in his stead.
text.NABRE: I saw all the living, those who move about under the sun, with the second youth who will succeed him.
text.DRB: Isaw all men alive, that walk under the sun, with the second youngman, which shall rise up for him.

text.NRSV: there was no end to all those people whom he led. Yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: There was no end to all the people, to all before whom he came, yet, they who should come later, would not rejoice in him,—surely, even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: There is no end of all the people, even of all that have been before them: they also that come after shall not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: There is no end to all this people, to all who were before them; yet the later generations will not have joy in him. This also is vanity and a chase after wind.
text.DRB: The number of the people, of all that have been before him is infinite: and they that shall be afterward, shall not rejoice in him. But this also is vanity, and affliction of spirit.

text.NRSV: Guard your steps when you go to the house of God; to draw near to listen is better than the sacrifice offered by fools; for they do not know how to keep from doing evil.
text.Rotherham: Keep thy foot, when thou goest unto the house of God, and be more ready to hear, than dullards to offer sacrifice,—for they make no acknowledgment of doing wrong.
text.KJV: Keep thy foot when thou goest to the house of God, and be more ready to hear, than to give the sacrifice of fools: for they consider not that they do evil.
text.NABRE: God is in heaven and you are on earth; therefore let your words be few.
text.DRB: Speak not any thing rashly, neither let thy heart be swift to utter a word before God. For God is in heaven, and thou upon the earth: therefore let thy words be few.

text.NRSV: Never be rash with your mouth, nor let your heart be quick to utter a word before God, for God is in heaven, and you upon earth; therefore let your words be few.
text.Rotherham: Be not rash with thy mouth, and, with thy heart, be not in haste to bring forth a word, before God,—for, God, is in the heavens, and, thou, upon the earth, for this cause, let thy words be few.
text.KJV: Be not rash with thy mouth, and let not thine heart be hasty to utter any thing before God: for God is in heaven, and thou upon earth: therefore let thy words be few.
text.NABRE: As dreams come along with many cares, so a fool’s voice along with a multitude of words.
text.DRB: Dreams do follow many cares, and in many words folly will be found.

text.NRSV: For dreams come with many cares, and a fool’s voice with many words.
text.Rotherham: For a dream cometh through the multitude of business,—and, the voice of a dullard, is with a multitude of words.
text.KJV: For a dream cometh through the multitude of business; and a fool’s voice is known by multitude of words.
text.NABRE: When you make a vow to God, delay not its fulfillment. For God has no pleasure in fools; fulfill what you have vowed.
text.DRB: If thou hast vowed any thing to God, defer not to pay it: for an unfaithful and foolish promise displeaseth him. But whatsoever thou hast vowed, pay it:

text.NRSV: When you make a vow to God, do not delay fulfilling it; for he has no pleasure in fools. Fulfill what you vow.
text.Rotherham: When thou vowest a vow unto God, do not defer to pay it, for there is no pleasure in dullards,—what thou vowest, pay!
text.KJV: When thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it; for he hath no pleasure in fools: pay that which thou hast vowed.
text.NABRE: It is better not to make a vow than make it and not fulfill it.
text.DRB: and it is much better not to vow, than after a vow not to perform the things promised.

text.NRSV: It is better that you should not vow than that you should vow and not fulfill it.
text.Rotherham: Better that thou shouldest not vow,—than vow, and not pay.
text.KJV: Better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay.
text.NABRE: Let not your utterances make you guilty, and say not before his representative, “It was a mistake.” Why should God be angered by your words and destroy the works of your hands?
text.DRB: Give not thy mouth to make thy flesh to sin: neither say thou before the Angel: There is no providence; lest perhaps God being wrath against thy words, dissipate all the works of thy hands.

text.NRSV: Do not let your mouth lead you into sin, and do not say before the messenger that it was a mistake; why should God be angry at your words, and destroy the work of your hands?
text.Rotherham: Do not let thy mouth cause thy flesh to sin,—neither say thou, before the messenger, that it was, a mistake,—wherefore should God be indignant at thy voice, and destroy the work of thy hands?
text.KJV: Suffer not thy mouth to cause thy flesh to sin; neither say thou before the angel, that it was an error: wherefore should God be angry at thy voice, and destroy the work of thine hands?
text.NABRE: Despite many dreams, futilities, and a multitude of words, fear God!
text.DRB: Where many dreams are, there are many vanities, and words innumerable: but do thou fear God.

text.NRSV: With many dreams come vanities and a multitude of words; but fear God.
text.Rotherham: For [it was done] amidst a multitude of dreams, and vanities, and many words,—but, towards God, be thou reverent.
text.KJV: For in the multitude of dreams and many words there are also divers vanities: but fear thou God.
text.NABRE: Gain and Loss of Goods. If you see oppression of the poor, and violation of rights and justice in the realm, do not be astonished by the fact, for the high official has another higher than he watching him and above these are others higher still—.
text.DRB: If thou shalt see the oppressions of the poor, and violent judgements, and justice to be subverted in the province, marvel not at this matter: because there is another higher than the high, and over these also there are others more eminent:

text.NRSV: If you see in a province the oppression of the poor and the violation of justice and right, do not be amazed at the matter; for the high official is watched by a higher, and there are yet higher ones over them.
text.Rotherham: If, the oppression of the poor, and the wresting of justice and righteousness, thou see in the province, do not be astonished over the matter,—for, one high above the highest, is watching, yea, the Most High, is over them.
text.KJV: If thou seest the oppression of the poor, and violent perverting of judgment and justice in a province, marvel not at the matter: for he that is higher than the highest regardeth; and there be higher than they.
text.NABRE: But profitable for a land in such circumstances is a king concerned about cultivation.
text.DRB: and besides, the King of all the earth reigneth over his servant.

text.NRSV: But all things considered, this is an advantage for a land: a king for a plowed field.
text.Rotherham: And, the profit of the earth, is, for all,—a king, by the field, is served.
text.KJV: Moreover the profit of the earth is for all: the king himself is served by the field.
text.NABRE: The covetous are never satisfied with money, nor lovers of wealth with their gain; so this too is vanity.
text.DRB: Acovetous man shall not be filled with money: and he that loveth riches, shall take no fruit of them: and this therefore is vanity.

text.NRSV: The lover of money will not be satisfied with money; nor the lover of wealth, with gain. This also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: He that loveth silver, shall not be satisfied with silver nor, he that loveth abundance, with revenue,—even this, was vanity.
text.KJV: He that loveth silver shall not be satisfied with silver; nor he that loveth abundance with increase: this is also vanity.
text.NABRE: Where there are great riches, there are also many to devour them. Of what use are they to the owner except as a feast for the eyes alone?
text.DRB: Where great riches are, there are also many that eat them. And what doth it profit the owner, but that he seeth the riches with his eyes?

text.NRSV: When goods increase, those who eat them increase; and what gain has their owner but to see them with his eyes?
text.Rotherham: When blessings are increased, increased are the eaters thereof,—what profit, then, to the owner of them saving the sight of his eyes?
text.KJV: When goods increase, they are increased that eat them: and what good is there to the owners thereof, saving the beholding of them with their eyes?
text.NABRE: Sleep is sweet to the laborer, whether there is little or much to eat; but the abundance of the rich allows them no sleep.
text.DRB: Sleep is sweet to him that worketh, whether he eat much or little: but the satiety of the rich doth not suffer him to sleep.

text.NRSV: Sweet is the sleep of laborers, whether they eat little or much; but the abundance of the rich will not let them sleep.
text.Rotherham: Sweet the sleep of the labourer, whether, little or much, he eat,—but, the surfeit of the rich man, will not suffer him to sleep.
text.KJV: The sleep of a labouring man is sweet, whether he eat little or much: but the abundance of the rich will not suffer him to sleep.
text.NABRE: This is a grievous evil which I have seen under the sun: riches hoarded by their owners to their own hurt.
text.DRB: There is also another very ill infirmity, which I have seen under the sun: riches kept to the hurt of the owner.

text.NRSV: There is a grievous ill that I have seen under the sun: riches were kept by their owner to his hurt,
text.Rotherham: Here was an incurable evil, I had seen under the sun, riches kept by the owner thereof, to his hurt;
text.KJV: There is a sore evil which I have seen under the sun, namely, riches kept for the owners thereof to their hurt.
text.NABRE: Should the riches be lost through some misfortune, they may have offspring when they have no means.
text.DRB: For they perish in very evil affliction: he hath begotten a son, which shall be in great poverty.

text.NRSV: and those riches were lost in a bad venture; though he is father of a son, he has nothing left.
text.Rotherham: and those riches perish, by being ill employed,—and though he begetteth a son, yet is there in his hand nothing at all.
text.KJV: But those riches perish by evil travail: and he begetteth a son, and there is nothing in his hand.
text.NABRE: As they came forth from their mother’s womb, so again shall they return, naked as they came, having nothing from their toil to bring with them.
text.DRB: As he came forth naked from his mother's womb, so shall he return, and shall take nothing away with him of his labour.

text.NRSV: As they came from their mother’s womb, so they shall go again, naked as they came; they shall take nothing for their toil, which they may carry away with them.
text.Rotherham: As he came from his mother’s womb, naked, he again departeth, as he came,—and, nothing, can he take of his toil, which he can carry in his hand.
text.KJV: As he came forth of his mother’s womb, naked shall he return to go as he came, and shall take nothing of his labour, which he may carry away in his hand.
text.NABRE: This too is a grievous evil, that they go just as they came. What then does it profit them to toil for the wind?
text.DRB: An infirmity utterly miserable; as he came, so shall he return. What doth it then profit him, that he hath laboured into the wind?

text.NRSV: This also is a grievous ill: just as they came, so shall they go; and what gain do they have from toiling for the wind,
text.Rotherham: Even this, moreover, is an incurable evil, altogether as he came, so, shall he depart,—what profit then shall he have who toileth for the wind?
text.KJV: And this also is a sore evil, that in all points as he came, so shall he go: and what profit hath he that hath laboured for the wind?
text.NABRE: All their days they eat in gloom with great vexation, sickness and resentment.
text.DRB: All the days of his life he eateth in darkness, and in misery, and in heaviness.

text.NRSV: besides spending all their days in darkness and grief, in much vexation and sickness and resentment?
text.Rotherham: Even all his days, [are spent] in darkness and mourning,—and he is very morose, and is sad and angry.
text.KJV: All his days also he eateth in darkness, and he hath much sorrow and wrath with his sickness.
text.NABRE: Here is what I see as good: It is appropriate to eat and drink and prosper from all the toil one toils at under the sun during the limited days of life God gives us; for this is our lot.
text.DRB: This therefore hath seemed good to me, that a man eat, and drink, and take joy of his labour, wherewith he hath laboured under the sun, the number of the days of his life, which God hath given him, and this is his portion.

text.NRSV: This is what I have seen to be good: it is fitting to eat and drink and find enjoyment in all the toil with which one toils under the sun the few days of the life God gives us; for this is our lot.
text.Rotherham: Lo! what, I myself, have seen—Better that it should be excellent to eat and to drink and to see blessedness, in all one’s toil wherein one toileth under the sun, for the number of the days of his life, in that God hath given it him, for, that, is his portion:
text.KJV: Behold that which I have seen: it is good and comely for one to eat and to drink, and to enjoy the good of all his labour that he taketh under the sun all the days of his life, which God giveth him: for it is his portion.
text.NABRE: Those to whom God gives riches and property, and grants power to partake of them, so that they receive their lot and find joy in the fruits of their toil: This is a gift from God.
text.DRB: And to every man, unto whom God hath given riches, and substance, and hath given him power to eat of them, and to enjoy his portion, and to rejoice of his labour: this is the gift of God.

text.NRSV: Likewise all to whom God gives wealth and possessions and whom he enables to enjoy them, and to accept their lot and find enjoyment in their toil—this is the gift of God.
text.Rotherham: yet, as regardeth every man, to whom God hath given wealth and goods, and granted him power to eat thereof, and to take his portion, and to find gladness in his toil, this, is, the gift of God.
text.KJV: Every man also to whom God hath given riches and wealth, and hath given him power to eat thereof, and to take his portion, and to rejoice in his labour; this is the gift of God.
text.NABRE: For they will hardly dwell on the shortness of life, because God lets them busy themselves with the joy of their heart.
text.DRB: For he shall not greatly remember the days of his life, because God doth occupy his heart with delights.

text.NRSV: For they will scarcely brood over the days of their lives, because God keeps them occupied with the joy of their hearts.
text.Rotherham: Though it be not much, let him remember the days of his life,—for, God, beareth witness, by the gladness of his heart.
text.KJV: For he shall not much remember the days of his life; because God answereth him in the joy of his heart.

text.NRSV: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, and it lies heavy upon humankind:
text.Rotherham: Here was an evil, I had seen under the sun,—and it is, common, among men:
text.KJV: There is an evil which I have seen under the sun, and it is common among men:
text.NABRE: There is another evil I have seen under the sun, and it weighs heavily upon humankind:
text.DRB: There is also another evil, which I have seen under the sun, and that frequent with men:

text.NRSV: those to whom God gives wealth, possessions, and honor, so that they lack nothing of all that they desire, yet God does not enable them to enjoy these things, but a stranger enjoys them. This is vanity; it is a grievous ill.
text.Rotherham: A man to whom God giveth riches and gains and honour, so that nothing doth he lack for his soul—of all that he craveth, and yet God doth not give him power to eat thereof, but, a man unknown, eateth it,—this, was vanity, and, an incurable evil, it was.
text.KJV: A man to whom God hath given riches, wealth, and honour, so that he wanteth nothing for his soul of all that he desireth, yet God giveth him not power to eat thereof, but a stranger eateth it: this is vanity, and it is an evil disease.
text.NABRE: There is one to whom God gives riches and property and honor, and who lacks nothing the heart could desire; yet God does not grant the power to partake of them, but a stranger devours them. This is vanity and a dire plague.
text.DRB: Aman to whom God hath given riches, and substance, and honour, and nothing is lacking to his soul of all things, which he desireth: neither doth God give him power to eat thereof: but a strange man shall eat it up. This is vanity and great misery.

text.NRSV: A man may beget a hundred children, and live many years, so that they are many; but if he does not enjoy life’s good things, and has no burial, I say that a stillborn child is better off than he.
text.Rotherham: Though a man should beget a hundred children, and live, many years, so that many should be the days of his years but, his own soul, should not be satisfied with the good, and he should not even have, a burial, I said, Better than he, is an untimely birth!
text.KJV: If a man beget an hundred children, and live many years, so that the days of his years be many, and his soul be not filled with good, and also that he have no burial; I say, that an untimely birth is better than he.
text.NABRE: Should one have a hundred children and live many years, no matter to what great age, still if one has not the full benefit of those goods, I proclaim that the child born dead, even if left unburied, is more fortunate.
text.DRB: If a man shall beget an hundred children, and shall live many years, and have many days of age, and his soul use not the goods of his substance, and he lack burial: of this man I pronounce, that the untimely born is better than he.

text.NRSV: For it comes into vanity and goes into darkness, and in darkness its name is covered;
text.Rotherham: For, in vain, it came in, and, in darkness, it departeth,—and, with darkness, its name, is covered:
text.KJV: For he cometh in with vanity, and departeth in darkness, and his name shall be covered with darkness.
text.NABRE: Though it came in vain and goes into darkness and its name is enveloped in darkness,
text.DRB: For he came in vain, and passeth to darkness, and his name shall be clean forgotten.

text.NRSV: moreover it has not seen the sun or known anything; yet it finds rest rather than he.
text.Rotherham: even the sun, it never saw, nor aught did it know,—more quietness, hath this than the other.
text.KJV: Moreover he hath not seen the sun, nor known any thing: this hath more rest than the other.
text.NABRE: though it has not seen the sun or known anything, yet the dead child has more peace.
text.DRB: He hath not seen the sun, nor known the distance of good and evil:

text.NRSV: Even though he should live a thousand years twice over, yet enjoy no good—do not all go to one place?
text.Rotherham: Even though one hath lived a thousand years twice told, yet, good, hath he not seen,—is it not, unto one place, that, all, are going?
text.KJV: Yea, though he live a thousand years twice told, yet hath he seen no good: do not all go to one place?
text.NABRE: Should such a one live twice a thousand years and not enjoy those goods, do not both go to the same place?
text.DRB: although he lived two thousand years, and hath not enjoyed good things: do not all things hasten to one place?

text.NRSV: All human toil is for the mouth, yet the appetite is not satisfied.
text.Rotherham: All the toil of man, is for his mouth,—though, even the desire, is not satisfied!
text.KJV: All the labour of man is for his mouth, and yet the appetite is not filled.
text.NABRE: All human toil is for the mouth, yet the appetite is never satisfied.
text.DRB: All the labour of man is in his mouth: but his soul shall not be filled.

text.NRSV: For what advantage have the wise over fools? And what do the poor have who know how to conduct themselves before the living?
text.Rotherham: For what profit hath the wise man, over the dullard? What can, the poor man, know—so as to walk before the living?
text.KJV: For what hath the wise more than the fool? what hath the poor, that knoweth to walk before the living?
text.NABRE: What profit have the wise compared to fools, or what profit have the lowly in knowing how to conduct themselves in life?
text.DRB: What hath the wiseman more than the fool? And what the poor man, but to pass thither, where life is?

text.NRSV: Better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the desire; this also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Better what the eyes behold, than the wandering of desire,—even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: Better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the desire: this is also vanity and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: “What the eyes see is better than what the desires wander after.” This also is vanity and a chase after wind.
text.DRB: Better it is to see that, which thou mayst covet, than to desire that, which thou canst not know. But this also is vanity, and presumption of spirit.

text.NRSV: Whatever has come to be has already been named, and it is known what human beings are, and that they cannot contend with one who is stronger than they.
text.Rotherham: Whatsoever one may be, long ago, was he called by his name, and it is known that it is—Son of Earth,—he cannot, therefore, contend with one stronger than he.
text.KJV: That which hath been is named already, and it is known that it is man: neither may he contend with him that is mightier than he.
text.NABRE: II. Qoheleth’s Conclusions Whatever is, was long ago given its name, and human nature is known; mortals cannot contend in judgment with One who is stronger.
text.DRB: He that shall be, his name is already called: and it is known, that he is a man, and cannot contend in judgement against a stronger than himself.

text.NRSV: The more words, the more vanity, and how is one the better?
text.Rotherham: Seeing there are things in abundance which make vanity abound, what profit hath man?
text.KJV: Seeing there be many things that increase vanity, what is man the better?
text.NABRE: For the more words, the more vanity; what profit is there for anyone?
text.DRB: There be many words, that have much vanity in disputing.

text.NRSV: For who knows what is good for mortals while they live the few days of their vain life, which they pass like a shadow? For who can tell them what will be after them under the sun?
text.Rotherham: For who knoweth what is good for a man throughout his life, for the number of the days of his life of vanity, seeing he will make them, like a shadow,—for who can tell a man, what shall be after him, under the sun?
text.KJV: For who knoweth what is good for man in this life, all the days of his vain life which he spendeth as a shadow? for who can tell a man what shall be after him under the sun?
text.NABRE: For who knows what is good for mortals in life, the limited days of their vain life, spent like a shadow? Because who can tell them what will come afterward under the sun?

text.NRSV: A good name is better than precious ointment, and the day of death, than the day of birth.
text.Rotherham: Better a name, than precious ointment,—and the day of death, than the day of one’s birth.
text.KJV: A good name is better than precious ointment; and the day of death than the day of one’s birth.
text.NABRE: A. No One Can Find Out the Best Way of Acting Chapter 7 Critique of Sages on the Day of Adversity A good name is better than good ointment, and the day of death than the day of birth.
text.DRB: What needeth a man to seek things greater than himself, whereas he is ignorant, what is profitable for him in his life, in the number of the days of his peregrination, and the time that passeth as a shadow? Or who can tell him what shall be after him under the sun?

text.NRSV: It is better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting; for this is the end of everyone, and the living will lay it to heart.
text.Rotherham: Better to go to the house of mourning, than to go to the house of banqueting, for, that, is the end of all men,—and, the living, should take it to his heart.
text.KJV: It is better to go to the house of mourning, than to go to the house of feasting: for that is the end of all men; and the living will lay it to his heart.
text.NABRE: It is better to go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting, For that is the end of every mortal, and the living should take it to heart.
text.DRB: Better is a good name than precious ointments: and the day of death, than the day of nativity.

text.NRSV: Sorrow is better than laughter, for by sadness of countenance the heart is made glad.
text.Rotherham: Better is grief than laughter,—for, by the marring of the face, amended is the heart.
text.KJV: Sorrow is better than laughter: for by the sadness of the countenance the heart is made better.
text.NABRE: Sorrow is better than laughter; when the face is sad, the heart grows wise.
text.DRB: It is better to go to the house of mourning, than to the house of banqueting, for in that the end of all men is signified, and he that liveth thinketh what shall be.

text.NRSV: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning; but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth.
text.Rotherham: The heart of the wise, is in the house of mourning, but, the heart of dullards, in the house of mirth.
text.KJV: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning; but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth.
text.NABRE: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, but the heart of fools is in the house of merriment.
