text.DRB: Anger is better than laughter: because by sadness of the countenance, the mind of the offender is corrected.

text.NRSV: It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise than to hear the song of fools.
text.Rotherham: Better to hear the rebuke of the wise, than for any man to hear the song of dullards,
text.KJV: It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise, than for a man to hear the song of fools.
text.NABRE: It is better to listen to the rebuke of the wise than to listen to the song of fools;
text.DRB: The heart of wisemen where sadness is, and the heart of fools where mirth.

text.NRSV: For like the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of fools; this also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: For, as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so, is the laughter of the dullard,—even this, then, was vanity.
text.KJV: For as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of the fool: this also is vanity.
text.NABRE: For as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the fool’s laughter. This also is vanity.
text.DRB: It is better to be rebuked of a wiseman, than to be deceived with the flattery of fools.

text.NRSV: Surely oppression makes the wise foolish, and a bribe corrupts the heart.
text.Rotherham: For, oppression, maddeneth the wise,—and a bribe, destroyeth the understanding.
text.KJV: Surely oppression maketh a wise man mad; and a gift destroyeth the heart.
text.NABRE: Extortion can make a fool out of the wise, and a bribe corrupts the heart.
text.DRB: Because as the sound of thorns burning under a pot, so the laughter of a fool: but this also is vanity.

text.NRSV: Better is the end of a thing than its beginning; the patient in spirit are better than the proud in spirit.
text.Rotherham: Better the latter end of a thing, than the beginning thereof,—Better a patient spirit, than a haughty spirit.
text.KJV: Better is the end of a thing than the beginning thereof: and the patient in spirit is better than the proud in spirit.
text.NABRE: Better is the end of a thing than its beginning; better is a patient spirit than a lofty one.
text.DRB: Oppression troubleth the wise, and shall destroy the strength of his heart.

text.NRSV: Do not be quick to anger, for anger lodges in the bosom of fools.
text.Rotherham: Do not be rash in thy spirit, to be indignant,—for, indignation, in the bosom of dullards, doth remain.
text.KJV: Be not hasty in thy spirit to be angry: for anger resteth in the bosom of fools.
text.NABRE: Do not let anger upset your spirit, for anger lodges in the bosom of a fool.
text.DRB: Better is the end of a speech, than the beginning. Better is the patient man than the arrogant.

text.NRSV: Do not say, “Why were the former days better than these?” For it is not from wisdom that you ask this.
text.Rotherham: Do not say, What hath happened, that, the former days, were better than these? for, not wisely, askest thou concerning this.
text.KJV: Say not thou, What is the cause that the former days were better than these? for thou dost not enquire wisely concerning this.
text.NABRE: Do not say: How is it that former times were better than these? For it is not out of wisdom that you ask about this.
text.DRB: Be not quickly angry, because anger resteth in the bosom of a fool.

text.NRSV: Wisdom is as good as an inheritance, an advantage to those who see the sun.
text.Rotherham: Good is wisdom, with an inheritance,—and a profit, to such as see the sun.
text.KJV: Wisdom is good with an inheritance: and by it there is profit to them that see the sun.
text.NABRE: Wisdom is as good as an inheritance and profitable to those who see the sun.
text.DRB: Say not: What is the cause thinkest thou that the former times were better than they are now? For this manner of question is foolish.

text.NRSV: For the protection of wisdom is like the protection of money, and the advantage of knowledge is that wisdom gives life to its possessor.
text.Rotherham: For, a protection, is wisdom, and, a protection, is silver,—but, the advantage of knowledge, is, that, wisdom, giveth life to the possessors thereof.
text.KJV: For wisdom is a defence, and money is a defence: but the excellency of knowledge is, that wisdom giveth life to them that have it.
text.NABRE: For the protection of wisdom is as the protection of money; and knowledge is profitable because wisdom gives life to those who possess it.
text.DRB: Wisdom with riches is more profitable, and doth more profit them that see the sun.

text.NRSV: Consider the work of God; who can make straight what he has made crooked?
text.Rotherham: Consider the work of God,—for who can straighten what he hath bent?
text.KJV: Consider the work of God: for who can make that straight, which he hath made crooked?
text.NABRE: Consider the work of God. Who can make straight what God has made crooked?
text.DRB: For as wisdom protecteth, so money protecteth. But learning and wisdom have this much more, that they give life to their owner.

text.NRSV: In the day of prosperity be joyful, and in the day of adversity consider; God has made the one as well as the other, so that mortals may not find out anything that will come after them.
text.Rotherham: In the day of prosperity, be joyful, but, in the day of misfortune, consider,—Even the one equally with the other, hath God made, to the end man might find out—after him—nothing.
text.KJV: In the day of prosperity be joyful, but in the day of adversity consider: God also hath set the one over against the other, to the end that man should find nothing after him.
text.NABRE: On a good day enjoy good things, and on an evil day consider: Both the one and the other God has made, so that no one may find the least fault with him.
text.DRB: Consider the works of God, that no man can correct whom he hath despised.

text.NRSV: In my vain life I have seen everything; there are righteous people who perish in their righteousness, and there are wicked people who prolong their life in their evildoing.
text.Rotherham: Everything, had I seen, in my days of vanity,—Here was a righteous man, perishing in his righteousness, and there was a lawless man, continuing long in his wickedness.
text.KJV: All things have I seen in the days of my vanity: there is a just man that perisheth in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man that prolongeth his life in his wickedness.
text.NABRE: Critique of Sages on Justice and Wickedness. I have seen all manner of things in my vain days: the just perishing in their justice, and the wicked living long in their wickedness.
text.DRB: In the good day enjoy good things, and beware before of the evil day. For as this, so that also hath God made, that man find not against him just complaints.

text.NRSV: Do not be overrighteous, and do not make yourself overwise; why should you destroy yourself?
text.Rotherham: Do not become so very righteous, neither count thyself wise beyond measure,—wherefore shouldst thou destroy thyself?
text.KJV: Be not righteous over much; neither make thyself over wise: why shouldest thou destroy thyself?
text.NABRE: “Be not just to excess, and be not overwise. Why work your own ruin?
text.DRB: These things also I saw in the days of my vanity: The just man perisheth in his justice, and the impious liveth a long time in his malice.

text.NRSV: Do not be overwicked, and do not be a fool; why should you die before your time?
text.Rotherham: Do not be so very lawless, neither become thou foolish,—wherefore shouldst thou die, before thy time?
text.KJV: Be not over much wicked, neither be thou foolish: why shouldest thou die before thy time?
text.NABRE: Be not wicked to excess, and be not foolish. Why should you die before your time?”
text.DRB: Be not just too much: neither be more wise than is necessary, lest thou become more dull.

text.NRSV: It is good that you should take hold of the one, without letting go of the other; for the one who fears God shall succeed with both.
text.Rotherham: It is well that thou shouldst lay fast hold of this, but, even from the other, do not withdraw thy hand,—for, he that revereth God, shall come forth out of them all.
text.KJV: It is good that thou shouldest take hold of this; yea, also from this withdraw not thine hand: for he that feareth God shall come forth of them all.
text.NABRE: It is good to hold to this rule, and not to let that one go; but the one who fears God will succeed with both.
text.DRB: Do not impiously much: and be not foolish, lest thou die not in thy time.

text.NRSV: Wisdom gives strength to the wise more than ten rulers that are in a city.
text.Rotherham: Wisdom, bringeth more strength to a wise man, than ten heroes, that are in the city.
text.KJV: Wisdom strengtheneth the wise more than ten mighty men which are in the city.
text.NABRE: Wisdom is a better defense for the wise than ten princes in the city,
text.DRB: It is good that thou hold up the just, yea and from him withdraw not thy hand: because he that feareth God, neglecteth nothing.

text.NRSV: Surely there is no one on earth so righteous as to do good without ever sinning.
text.Rotherham: For, as for men, there is none righteous in the earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not.
text.KJV: For there is not a just man upon earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not.
text.NABRE: yet there is no one on earth so just as to do good and never sin.
text.DRB: Wisdom hath strengthened the wise above ten Princes of the city.

text.NRSV: Do not give heed to everything that people say, or you may hear your servant cursing you;
text.Rotherham: Moreover, not to all the words which men speak, do thou apply thy heart,—lest thou hear thine own servant reviling thee!
text.KJV: Also take no heed unto all words that are spoken; lest thou hear thy servant curse thee:
text.NABRE: Do not give your heart to every word that is spoken; you may hear your servant cursing you,
text.DRB: For there is no just man in the earth, that doth good, and sinneth not.

text.NRSV: your heart knows that many times you have yourself cursed others.
text.Rotherham: For truly, many times, thy heart knoweth,—that, even thou thyself, hast reviled others.
text.KJV: For oftentimes also thine own heart knoweth that thou thyself likewise hast cursed others.
text.NABRE: for your heart knows that you have many times cursed others.
text.DRB: But to all words also that are spoken, do not apply thy heart: lest perhaps thou hear thy servant cursing thee.

text.NRSV: All this I have tested by wisdom; I said, “I will be wise,” but it was far from me.
text.Rotherham: All this, have I proved by wisdom,—I said, I will be wise, but, that, was far from me.
text.KJV: All this have I proved by wisdom: I said, I will be wise; but it was far from me.
text.NABRE: All these things I probed in wisdom. I said, “I will acquire wisdom”; but it was far beyond me.
text.DRB: For thy conscience knoweth, that thou also hast cursed others.

text.NRSV: That which is, is far off, and deep, very deep; who can find it out?
text.Rotherham: Far away, is that which hath been,—and deep, deep, who can find it out?
text.KJV: That which is far off, and exceeding deep, who can find it out?
text.NABRE: What exists is far-reaching; it is deep, very deep: Who can find it out?
text.DRB: I have proved all things in wisdom. I have said: I will become wise, & it departed farther from me

text.NRSV: I turned my mind to know and to search out and to seek wisdom and the sum of things, and to know that wickedness is folly and that foolishness is madness.
text.Rotherham: Resolved, I, in my heart, to know and search out, and to seek wisdom, and a conclusion,—and to know lawlessness [to be] stupidity, and folly to be madness.
text.KJV: I applied mine heart to know, and to search, and to seek out wisdom, and the reason of things, and to know the wickedness of folly, even of foolishness and madness:
text.NABRE: I turned my heart toward knowledge; I sought and pursued wisdom and its design, and I recognized that wickedness is foolishness and folly is madness.
text.DRB: much more than it was: and a deep profundity, who shall find it?

text.NRSV: I found more bitter than death the woman who is a trap, whose heart is snares and nets, and whose hands are fetters; one who pleases God escapes her, but the sinner is taken by her.
text.Rotherham: I, could indeed find, to be, more bitter than death, the woman, whose heart is, snares and nets, and her hands, bonds,—whoso is pleasing before God, shall escape from her, but, he that sinneth, shall be captured by her.
text.KJV: And I find more bitter than death the woman, whose heart is snares and nets, and her hands as bands: whoso pleaseth God shall escape from her; but the sinner shall be taken by her.
text.NABRE: Critique of Advice on Women. More bitter than death I find the woman who is a hunter’s trap, whose heart is a snare, whose hands are prison bonds. The one who pleases God will be delivered from her, but the one who displeases will be entrapped by her.
text.DRB: I have viewed all things with my mind, that I might know, and consider, and might seek wisdom and reason: and that I might know the impiety of the fool, and the error of the imprudent:

text.NRSV: See, this is what I found, says the Teacher, adding one thing to another to find the sum,
text.Rotherham: See! this, have I found, saith the Proclaimer [counting] one by one, to find a conclusion;
text.KJV: Behold, this have I found, saith the preacher, counting one by one, to find out the account:
text.NABRE: See, this have I found, says Qoheleth, adding one to one to find the sum.
text.DRB: and I have found that a woman is more bitter than death, who is the snare of hunters, and her heart a net, her hands are bands. He that pleaseth God, will avoid her: but he that is a sinner, will be caught of her.

text.NRSV: which my mind has sought repeatedly, but I have not found. One man among a thousand I found, but a woman among all these I have not found.
text.Rotherham: what my soul still sought, yet I found not,—one man out of a thousand, have I found, but, a woman among all these, have I not found.
text.KJV: Which yet my soul seeketh, but I find not: one man among a thousand have I found; but a woman among all those have I not found.
text.NABRE: What my soul still seeks and has yet to find is this: “One man out of a thousand have I found, but a woman among them all I have not found.”
text.DRB: Lo this have I found, said Ecclesiastes, one thing and another, that I might find reason,

text.NRSV: See, this alone I found, that God made human beings straightforward, but they have devised many schemes.
text.Rotherham: Only, see, this, have I found, That God made man upright, but, they, have sought out many devices.
text.KJV: Lo, this only have I found, that God hath made man upright; but they have sought out many inventions.
text.NABRE: But this alone I have found: God made humankind honest, but they have pursued many designs.
text.DRB: which yet my soul seeketh, and I have not found it. A man of a thousand I have found one, a woman of all I have not found.

text.NRSV: Who is like the wise? And who knows the interpretation of a thing? Wisdom illumines the face, and the hardness of one’s countenance is changed.
text.Rotherham: Who is really a wise man, and who knoweth the interpretation of a thing? The wisdom of a man, lighteth up his countenance, but, by defiance of countenance, one is disfigured.
text.KJV: Who is as the wise man? and who knoweth the interpretation of a thing? a man’s wisdom maketh his face to shine, and the boldness of his face shall be changed.
text.NABRE: Chapter 8 Critique of Advice to Heed Authority Who is like the wise person, and who knows the explanation of things? Wisdom illumines the face and transforms a grim countenance.
text.DRB: The wisdom of a man shineth in his countenance, and the most mighty will change his face.

text.NRSV: Keep the king’s command because of your sacred oath.
text.Rotherham: I [said], The bidding of the king, observe thou, even out of regard to the oath of God.
text.KJV: I counsel thee to keep the king’s commandment, and that in regard of the oath of God.
text.NABRE: Observe the command of the king, in view of your oath to God.
text.DRB: Iobserve the mouth of the king, and the precepts of the oath of God.

text.NRSV: Do not be terrified; go from his presence, do not delay when the matter is unpleasant, for he does whatever he pleases.
text.Rotherham: Not rashly from his presence, shouldst thou go: do not take thy stand in a vexatious thing,—for, whatsoever he pleaseth, he will do.
text.KJV: Be not hasty to go out of his sight: stand not in an evil thing; for he doeth whatsoever pleaseth him.
text.NABRE: Be not hasty to withdraw from the king; do not persist in an unpleasant situation, for he does whatever he pleases.
text.DRB: Hasten not to depart from his face, nor continue thou in an evil work: because all that he pleaseth he will do,

text.NRSV: For the word of the king is powerful, and who can say to him, “What are you doing?”
text.Rotherham: Where the word of a king is, there is power,—who then may say to him, What wouldst thou do?
text.KJV: Where the word of a king is, there is power: and who may say unto him, What doest thou?
text.NABRE: His word is sovereign, and who can say to him, “What are you doing?”
text.DRB: and his word is full of power: neither can any man say to him. Why dost thou so:

text.NRSV: Whoever keeps a command will know no evil thing, and the wise mind will know the time and way.
text.Rotherham: He that observeth the commandment, will not notice a vexatious thing,—and, of time and manner, will the heart of the wise take note.
text.KJV: Whoso keepeth the commandment shall feel no evil thing: and a wise man’s heart discerneth both time and judgment.
text.NABRE: “Whoever observes a command knows no harm, and the wise heart knows times and judgments.”
text.DRB: He that keepeth the precept, shall find no evil. The heart of a wiseman understandeth time and answer.

text.NRSV: For every matter has its time and way, although the troubles of mortals lie heavy upon them.
text.Rotherham: For, to every pursuit, there is a time and a manner,—when, the vexation of man, is great concerning it.
text.KJV: Because to every purpose there is time and judgment, therefore the misery of man is great upon him.
text.NABRE: Yes, there is a time and a judgment for everything. But it is a great evil for mortals
text.DRB: There is a time for all business, and opportunity, and much affliction of man:

text.NRSV: Indeed, they do not know what is to be, for who can tell them how it will be?
text.Rotherham: For there is no one who knoweth what shall be, for, when it shall be, who will tell him?
text.KJV: For he knoweth not that which shall be: for who can tell him when it shall be?
text.NABRE: that they are ignorant of what is to come; for who will make known to them how it will be?
text.DRB: because he is ignorant of things past, and things to come he can know by no messenger.

text.NRSV: No one has power over the wind to restrain the wind, or power over the day of death; there is no discharge from the battle, nor does wickedness deliver those who practice it.
text.Rotherham: No man, hath power over the spirit, to retain the spirit, and, none, hath power over the day of death, and there is no furlough in war,—neither shall lawlessness deliver them who are given thereto.
text.KJV: There is no man that hath power over the spirit to retain the spirit; neither hath he power in the day of death: and there is no discharge in that war; neither shall wickedness deliver those that are given to it.
text.NABRE: No one is master of the breath of life so as to retain it, and none has mastery of the day of death. There is no exemption in wartime, nor does wickedness deliver those who practice it.
text.DRB: It is not in man's power to prohibit the spirit, neither hath he power in the day of death, neither is he suffered to rest when war is at hand, neither shall impiety save the impious.

text.NRSV: All this I observed while applying my mind to all that is done under the sun, while one person exercises authority over another to the other’s hurt.
text.Rotherham: All this, had I seen, and tried to apply my heart to every work which was done under the sun,—at such time as one man had power over another man, to his hurt.
text.KJV: All this have I seen, and applied my heart unto every work that is done under the sun: there is a time wherein one man ruleth over another to his own hurt.
text.NABRE: All these things I saw and I applied my heart to every work that is done under the sun, while one person tyrannizes over another for harm.
text.DRB: All these things I have considered, and gave my heart on all the works that are done under the sun. Sometime man ruleth over man to his own hurt.

text.NRSV: Then I saw the wicked buried; they used to go in and out of the holy place, and were praised in the city where they had done such things. This also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: And, thereupon, I considered the lawless when buried, when they had entered, [their graves], that, from the place of the Holy One, they used to go and boast in the city that they had so done,—even this, was vanity.
text.KJV: And so I saw the wicked buried, who had come and gone from the place of the holy, and they were forgotten in the city where they had so done: this is also vanity.
text.NABRE: The Problem of Retribution. Meanwhile I saw the wicked buried. They would come and go from the holy place. But those were forgotten in the city who had acted justly. This also is vanity.
text.DRB: Isaw the impious buried: who also when they yet lived, were in holy place, and were praised in the city as men of just works. But this also is vanity.

text.NRSV: Because sentence against an evil deed is not executed speedily, the human heart is fully set to do evil.
text.Rotherham: Because sentence against a wicked work is not executed speedily—on this account, the heart of the sons of men is fully set within them, to commit wickedness.
text.KJV: Because sentence against an evil work is not executed speedily, therefore the heart of the sons of men is fully set in them to do evil.
text.NABRE: Because the sentence against an evil deed is not promptly executed, the human heart is filled with the desire to commit evil—
text.DRB: Because sentence is not speedily pronounced against the evil, the children of men commit evils without any fear.

text.NRSV: Though sinners do evil a hundred times and prolong their lives, yet I know that it will be well with those who fear God, because they stand in awe before him;
text.Rotherham: Though a sinner be committing wickedness a hundred times, and continuing long in his own way, yet I surely know that it shall be well to them who revere God, who stand in awe before him;
text.KJV: Though a sinner do evil an hundred times, and his days be prolonged, yet surely I know that it shall be well with them that fear God, which fear before him:
text.NABRE: because the sinner does evil a hundred times and survives. Though indeed I know that it shall be well with those who fear God, for their reverence toward him;
text.DRB: But yet a sinner by this that he doth evil an hundred times, & by patience is borne withal, I know that it shall be good to them that fear God, which dread his face.

text.NRSV: but it will not be well with the wicked, neither will they prolong their days like a shadow, because they do not stand in awe before God.
text.Rotherham: but, well, shall it not be to the lawless man, neither shall he lengthen out his days like a shadow,—because he standeth not in awe before God.
text.KJV: But it shall not be well with the wicked, neither shall he prolong his days, which are as a shadow; because he feareth not before God.
text.NABRE: and that it shall not be well with the wicked, who shall not prolong their shadowy days, for their lack of reverence toward God.
text.DRB: Let there be no good to the impious, neither let his days be prolonged, but as a shadow let them pass, that fear not the face of our Lord.

text.NRSV: There is a vanity that takes place on earth, that there are righteous people who are treated according to the conduct of the wicked, and there are wicked people who are treated according to the conduct of the righteous. I said that this also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: Here was a vain thing which was done upon the earth—that there were righteous men unto whom it happened according to the work of the lawless, and there were lawless men, unto whom it happened according to the work of the righteous,—I said, that, even this, was vanity.
text.KJV: There is a vanity which is done upon the earth; that there be just men, unto whom it happeneth according to the work of the wicked; again, there be wicked men, to whom it happeneth according to the work of the righteous: I said that this also is vanity.
text.NABRE: This is a vanity that occurs on earth: There are those who are just but are treated as though they had done evil, and those who are wicked but are treated as though they had done justly. This, too, I say is vanity.
text.DRB: There is also another vanity, which is done upon the earth. There are just men, to whom evils happen, as though they had done the works of the impious: and there are impious men, which are so secure, as though they had the deeds of the just. But this also I judge most vain.

text.NRSV: So I commend enjoyment, for there is nothing better for people under the sun than to eat and drink and enjoy themselves, for this will go with them in their toil through the days of life that God gives them under the sun.
text.Rotherham: Then extolled I, gladness, in that there was nothing better for a man, under the sun, than to eat and to drink, and to be glad,—since, that, should tarry with him in his toil, for the days of his life which God had given him under the sun.
text.KJV: Then I commended mirth, because a man hath no better thing under the sun, than to eat, and to drink, and to be merry: for that shall abide with him of his labour the days of his life, which God giveth him under the sun.
text.NABRE: Therefore I praised joy, because there is nothing better for mortals under the sun than to eat and to drink and to be joyful; this will accompany them in their toil through the limited days of life God gives them under the sun.
text.DRB: Itherefore have praised mirth, that there was no good thing for a man under the sun, but that he should eat, and drink, and be glad: and this only he should take away with him of his labour in the days of his life, which God hath given him under the sun.

text.NRSV: When I applied my mind to know wisdom, and to see the business that is done on earth, how one’s eyes see sleep neither day nor night,
text.Rotherham: When I gave my heart, to know wisdom, and to consider the business that was done upon the earth, then surely, by day and by night, there was one who suffered not his eyes, to sleep.
text.KJV: When I applied mine heart to know wisdom, and to see the business that is done upon the earth: (for also there is that neither day nor night seeth sleep with his eyes:)
text.NABRE: I applied my heart to know wisdom and to see the business that is done on earth, though neither by day nor by night do one’s eyes see sleep,
text.DRB: And I have set my heart to know wisdom, and to understand the distraction that is in the earth: There is a man that days and nights taketh no sleep with his eyes.

text.NRSV: then I saw all the work of God, that no one can find out what is happening under the sun. However much they may toil in searching, they will not find it out; even though those who are wise claim to know, they cannot find it out.
text.Rotherham: Then I considered all the work of God, that man could not find out the work that was done under the sun, inasmuch as man toileth in seeking and yet cannot find,—yea, even though the wise man should say he knoweth, yet can he not find it out.
text.KJV: Then I beheld all the work of God, that a man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun: because though a man labour to seek it out, yet he shall not find it; yea further; though a wise man think to know it, yet shall he not be able to find it.
text.NABRE: and I saw all the work of God: No mortal can find out the work that is done under the sun. However much mortals may toil in searching, no one finds it out; and even if the wise claim to know, they are unable to find it out.
text.DRB: And I understood that man can find no reason of all those works of God, that are done under the sun: and the more he shall labour to seek, so much the less he can find: yea if the wiseman shall say that he knoweth, he is not able to find it.

text.NRSV: All this I laid to heart, examining it all, how the righteous and the wise and their deeds are in the hand of God; whether it is love or hate one does not know. Everything that confronts them
text.Rotherham: For, unto all this, I applied my heart, and, my heart, considered all this, that, the righteous and the wise and their servants, were in the hand of God,—neither love nor hatred, could any man know, every one, was before Him.
text.KJV: For all this I considered in my heart even to declare all this, that the righteous, and the wise, and their works, are in the hand of God: no man knoweth either love or hatred by all that is before them.
text.NABRE: B. No One Knows the Future Chapter 9 All this I have kept in my heart and all this I examined: The just, the wise, and their deeds are in the hand of God. Love from hatred mortals cannot tell; both are before them.
text.DRB: All these things have I discoursed in my heart, that I might curiously understand them: there are just men and wise: and their works are in the hand of God: and yet man knoweth not whether he be worthy of love or hatred:

text.NRSV: is the same for all, since one fate comes to all, to the righteous and the wicked, to the good and the evil, to the clean and the unclean, to those who sacrifice and those who do not sacrifice. As are the good, so are the sinners; and those who swear are like those who shun an oath.
text.Rotherham: Every one, was like every one else, one destiny, had the righteous and the lawless, the good and the pure and the impure, and he that sacrificed, and he that did not sacrifice,—as the good man, so, the sinner, he that took an oath, as he who, of an oath, stood in fear.
text.KJV: All things come alike to all: there is one event to the righteous, and to the wicked; to the good and to the clean, and to the unclean; to him that sacrificeth, and to him that sacrificeth not: as is the good, so is the sinner; and he that sweareth, as he that feareth an oath.
text.NABRE: Everything is the same for everybody: the same lot for the just and the wicked, for the good, for the clean and the unclean, for the one who offers sacrifice and the one who does not. As it is for the good, so it is for the sinner; as it is for the one who takes an oath, so it is for the one who fears an oath.
text.DRB: but all things are reserved uncertain for the time to come, because all things do equally chance to the just and impious, to the good and the evil, to the clean and unclean, to him that immolateth victims, and him that contemneth sacrifices. As the good, so also is the sinner: as the perjured, so he also that sweareth truth.

text.NRSV: This is an evil in all that happens under the sun, that the same fate comes to everyone. Moreover, the hearts of all are full of evil; madness is in their hearts while they live, and after that they go to the dead.
text.Rotherham: This was a misfortune in all that was done under the sun, that, one destiny, had they all,—yea also, the heart of the sons of men, was full of wickedness, and, madness, was in their heart, while they lived, and, after that, [they went] unto the dead.
text.KJV: This is an evil among all things that are done under the sun, that there is one event unto all: yea, also the heart of the sons of men is full of evil, and madness is in their heart while they live, and after that they go to the dead.
text.NABRE: Among all the things that are done under the sun, this is the worst, that there is one lot for all. Hence the hearts of human beings are filled with evil, and madness is in their hearts during life; and afterward—to the dead!
text.DRB: This is a very evil thing among all which are done under the sun, that the same things chance to all men. Whereby also the hearts of the children of men are filled with malice, and with contempt in their life, and after that they shall be brought down to hell.

text.NRSV: But whoever is joined with all the living has hope, for a living dog is better than a dead lion.
text.Rotherham: For, whosoever was united to all the living, for him, there was hope,—inasmuch as, a living dog, fared better than a dead lion.
text.KJV: For to him that is joined to all the living there is hope: for a living dog is better than a dead lion.
text.NABRE: For whoever is chosen among all the living has hope: “A live dog is better off than a dead lion.”
text.DRB: There is no man that may live always, and that can have confidence of this thing: better is a dog living than a lion dead.

text.NRSV: The living know that they will die, but the dead know nothing; they have no more reward, and even the memory of them is lost.
text.Rotherham: For, the living, knew that they should die,—but, the dead, knew not, anything, neither had they any longer a reward, because forgotten was their memory.
text.KJV: For the living know that they shall die: but the dead know not any thing, neither have they any more a reward; for the memory of them is forgotten.
text.NABRE: For the living know that they are to die, but the dead no longer know anything. There is no further recompense for them, because all memory of them is lost.
text.DRB: For the living know that they shall die, but the dead know nothing more, neither have they reward any more: because the memory of them is forgotten.

text.NRSV: Their love and their hate and their envy have already perished; never again will they have any share in all that happens under the sun.
text.Rotherham: Both their love and their hatred and their envy, already had perished,—and, portion, had they none any longer, unto times age-abiding, in aught that was done under the sun.
text.KJV: Also their love, and their hatred, and their envy, is now perished; neither have they any more a portion for ever in any thing that is done under the sun.
text.NABRE: For them, love and hatred and rivalry have long since perished. Never again will they have part in anything that is done under the sun.
text.DRB: Love also and hatred, and envies have perished together, neither have they part in this world, and in the work that is done under the sun.

text.NRSV: Go, eat your bread with enjoyment, and drink your wine with a merry heart; for God has long ago approved what you do.
text.Rotherham: Go thy way—eat, with gladness, thy food, and drink, with a happy heart, thy wine,—when already God is well pleased with thy works.
text.KJV: Go thy way, eat thy bread with joy, and drink thy wine with a merry heart; for God now accepteth thy works.
text.NABRE: Go, eat your bread with joy and drink your wine with a merry heart, because it is now that God favors your works.
text.DRB: Go therefore and eat thy bread in joy, & drink thy wine with gladness: because thy works please God.

text.NRSV: Let your garments always be white; do not let oil be lacking on your head.
text.Rotherham: Continually, let thy garments be white,—and, ointment upon thy head, let it not be lacking.
text.KJV: Let thy garments be always white; and let thy head lack no ointment.
text.NABRE: At all times let your garments be white, and spare not the perfume for your head.
text.DRB: At all time let thy garments be white, and let not oil fall from off thy head.

text.NRSV: Enjoy life with the wife whom you love, all the days of your vain life that are given you under the sun, because that is your lot in life and in your toil at which you toil under the sun.
text.Rotherham: Enjoy life, with thy wife whom thou lovest, all the days of thy life of vanity, which he hath given thee under the sun, all thy days of vanity,—for, that, is thy portion in life, and in thy toil wherewith, thou, art toiling under the sun.
text.KJV: Live joyfully with the wife whom thou lovest all the days of the life of thy vanity, which he hath given thee under the sun, all the days of thy vanity: for that is thy portion in this life, and in thy labour which thou takest under the sun.
text.NABRE: Enjoy life with the wife you love, all the days of the vain life granted you under the sun. This is your lot in life, for the toil of your labors under the sun.
text.DRB: Enjoy life, with thy wife whom thou lovest, all the days of the life of thy instability, which are given to thee under the sun, all the time of thy vanity: for this is the portion in life, and in thy labour, wherewith thou labourest under the sun.

text.NRSV: Whatever your hand finds to do, do with your might; for there is no work or thought or knowledge or wisdom in Sheol, to which you are going.
text.Rotherham: Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, with thy might, do,—for there is no work nor calculation nor knowledge nor wisdom, in hades, whither, thou, art going.
text.KJV: Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with thy might; for there is no work, nor device, nor knowledge, nor wisdom, in the grave, whither thou goest.
text.NABRE: Anything you can turn your hand to, do with what power you have; for there will be no work, no planning, no knowledge, no wisdom in Sheol where you are going.
text.DRB: Whatsoever thy hand is able to do, work it instantly: for neither work, nor reason, nor wisdom, nor knowledge shall be in hell, whither thou dost hasten.

text.NRSV: Again I saw that under the sun the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the intelligent, nor favor to the skillful; but time and chance happen to them all.
text.Rotherham: I again saw under the sun, that not, to the swift, was the race, nor, to the strong, the battle, nay! nor, to the wise, food, nor yet, to the intelligent, riches, nor, even to the well-informed, comeliness,—for, time and accident, happened to them all.
text.KJV: I returned, and saw under the sun, that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, neither yet bread to the wise, nor yet riches to men of understanding, nor yet favour to men of skill; but time and chance happeneth to them all.
text.NABRE: The Time of Misfortune Is Not Known. Again I saw under the sun that the race is not won by the swift, nor the battle by the valiant, nor a livelihood by the wise, nor riches by the shrewd, nor favor by the experts; for a time of misfortune comes to all alike.
text.DRB: Iturned me to another thing, and I saw under the sun, that neither running is of the swift, nor war of the strong, nor bread of the wise, nor riches of the learned, nor grace of the artificers: but time and chance in all.

text.NRSV: For no one can anticipate the time of disaster. Like fish enumerated in a fatal net, and like birds caught in a snare, so mortals are snared at a time of calamity, when it suddenly falls upon them.
text.Rotherham: For, indeed, man could not know his own time, like fishes which were caught in a cruel net, and like little birds which were caught in a trap,—like them, were ensnared the sons of men, by a time of misfortune, when it fell upon them suddenly.
text.KJV: For man also knoweth not his time: as the fishes that are taken in an evil net, and as the birds that are caught in the snare; so are the sons of men snared in an evil time, when it falleth suddenly upon them.
text.NABRE: Human beings no more know their own time than fish taken in the fatal net or birds trapped in the snare; like these, mortals are caught when an evil time suddenly falls upon them.
text.DRB: Man knoweth not his own end: but as fishes are taken with the hook, and as birds are caught with the snare, so men are taken in the evil time, when it shall suddenly come upon them.

text.NRSV: I have also seen this example of wisdom under the sun, and it seemed great to me.
text.Rotherham: Even this, had I seen of wisdom, under the sun,—and, of great import, was the same unto me:—
text.KJV: This wisdom have I seen also under the sun, and it seemed great unto me:
text.NABRE: The Uncertain Future and the Sages. On the other hand I saw this wise deed under the sun, which I thought magnificent.
text.DRB: This wisdom also I have seen under the sun and have proved it to be very great:

text.NRSV: There was a little city with few people in it. A great king came against it and besieged it, building great siegeworks against it.
text.Rotherham: A little city, and men therein few,—and there came against it a great king, and surrounded it, and built against it large siege-works;
text.KJV: There was a little city, and few men within it; and there came a great king against it, and besieged it, and built great bulwarks against it:
text.NABRE: Against a small city with few inhabitants advanced a mighty king, who surrounded it and threw up great siegeworks about it.
text.DRB: Alittle city, and few men in it: there came against it a great king, and compassed it, and builded forts round about, and the siege was perfited.

text.NRSV: Now there was found in it a poor wise man, and he by his wisdom delivered the city. Yet no one remembered that poor man.
text.Rotherham: but there was found therein, a man, poor [but] wise, and, he, delivered the city by his wisdom,—yet, no one, remembered that poor man.
text.KJV: Now there was found in it a poor wise man, and he by his wisdom delivered the city; yet no man remembered that same poor man.
text.NABRE: But in the city lived a man who, though poor, was wise, and he delivered it through his wisdom. Yet no one remembered this poor man.
text.DRB: And there was found in it a man poor and wise, and he delivered the city by his wisdom, and no man afterward remembered that poorman.

text.NRSV: So I said, “Wisdom is better than might; yet the poor man’s wisdom is despised, and his words are not heeded.”
text.Rotherham: Then said, I, Better is wisdom than strength,—although, the wisdom of the poor man, be despised, and, his words, not heard.
text.KJV: Then said I, Wisdom is better than strength: nevertheless the poor man’s wisdom is despised, and his words are not heard.
text.NABRE: Though I had said, “Wisdom is better than force,” yet the wisdom of the poor man is despised and his words go unheeded.
text.DRB: And I said, that wisdom is better than strength: how then was the wisdom of the poorman contemned, & his words were not heard?

text.NRSV: The quiet words of the wise are more to be heeded than the shouting of a ruler among fools.
text.Rotherham: The words of the wise, in quietness, are heard,—beyond the outcry of one who ruleth over dullards.
text.KJV: The words of wise men are heard in quiet more than the cry of him that ruleth among fools.
text.NABRE: The quiet words of the wise are better heeded than the shout of a ruler of fools.
text.DRB: The words of the wise are heard in silence, more than the cry of a Prince among fools.

text.NRSV: Wisdom is better than weapons of war, but one sinner destroys much good.
text.Rotherham: Better is wisdom, than weapons of war,—but, one sinner, may destroy much good.
text.KJV: Wisdom is better than weapons of war: but one sinner destroyeth much good.
text.NABRE: Wisdom is better than weapons of war, but one bungler destroys much good.
text.DRB: Better is wisdom, than weapons of war: and he that shall offend in one point, shall lose many good things.

text.NRSV: Dead flies make the perfumer’s ointment give off a fetid odor; so a little folly outweighs wisdom and honor.
text.Rotherham: Dead flies, cause to stink [and] ferment, the oil of the perfumer,—More costly than wisdom or honour, is a little folly.
text.KJV: Dead flies cause the ointment of the apothecary to send forth a stinking savour: so doth a little folly him that is in reputation for wisdom and honour.
text.NABRE: Dead flies corrupt and spoil the perfumer’s oil; more weighty than wisdom or wealth is a little folly!
text.DRB: Flies dying mar the sweetness of ointment. Wisdom and glory is more precious, than a little and temporal folly.

text.NRSV: The heart of the wise inclines to the right, but the heart of a fool to the left.
text.Rotherham: The sense of the wise, is on his right hand,—But, the sense of the dullard, on his left:
text.KJV: A wise man’s heart is at his right hand; but a fool’s heart at his left.
text.NABRE: The wise heart turns to the right; the foolish heart to the left.
text.DRB: The heart of a wiseman is in his right hand, and the heart of a fool is in his left hand.

text.NRSV: Even when fools walk along the road, they lack sense, and they show everyone that they are fools.
text.Rotherham: Yea, even by the way, as the foolish man walketh along, his sense faileth him—and he telleth everyone that, foolish, is he!
text.KJV: Yea also, when he that is a fool walketh by the way, his wisdom faileth him, and he saith to every one that he is a fool.
text.NABRE: Even when walking in the street the fool, lacking understanding, calls everyone a fool.
text.DRB: Yea and the fool walking in the way, whereas himself is unwise, esteemeth all men fools.

text.NRSV: If the anger of the ruler rises against you, do not leave your post, for calmness will lay great offenses to rest.
text.Rotherham: If, the spirit of a ruler, riseth up against thee, thy place, do not leave, for gentleness, pacifieth such as have greatly erred.
text.KJV: If the spirit of the ruler rise up against thee, leave not thy place; for yielding pacifieth great offences.
text.NABRE: Should the anger of a ruler burst upon you, do not yield your place; for calmness abates great offenses.
text.DRB: If the spirit of him that hath power, ascend upon thee, leave not thy place: because carefulness will make the greatest sins to cease.

text.NRSV: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, as it were an error proceeding from the ruler:
text.Rotherham: Here was a misfortune I had seen under the sun,—a veritable mistake that was going forth from the presence of one who had power:
text.KJV: There is an evil which I have seen under the sun, as an error which proceedeth from the ruler:
text.NABRE: I have seen under the sun another evil, like a mistake that proceeds from a tyrant:
text.DRB: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, as it were by error proceeding from the face of the Prince:

text.NRSV: folly is set in many high places, and the rich sit in a low place.
text.Rotherham: Folly placed in great dignity,—while, the rich, in a low place, took their seat:
text.KJV: Folly is set in great dignity, and the rich sit in low place.
text.NABRE: a fool put in high position, while the great and the rich sit in lowly places.
text.DRB: a fool set in high dignity, and the rich to sit beneath.

text.NRSV: I have seen slaves on horseback, and princes walking on foot like slaves.
text.Rotherham: I had seen, servants, upon horses,—and, rulers, walking like servants, on the ground.
text.KJV: I have seen servants upon horses, and princes walking as servants upon the earth.
text.NABRE: I have seen slaves on horseback, while princes went on foot like slaves.
text.DRB: I have seen servants upon horses: and Princes walking on the ground as servants.

text.NRSV: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it; and whoever breaks through a wall will be bitten by a snake.
text.Rotherham: He that diggeth a pit, thereinto, may fall,—and, he that breaketh through a hedge, there may bite him a serpent.
text.KJV: He that diggeth a pit shall fall into it; and whoso breaketh an hedge, a serpent shall bite him.
text.NABRE: Whoever digs a pit may fall into it, and whoever breaks through a wall, a snake may bite.
text.DRB: He that diggeth a pit, shall fall into it: and he that breaketh the hedge, a serpent shall bite him.

text.NRSV: Whoever quarries stones will be hurt by them; and whoever splits logs will be endangered by them.
text.Rotherham: He that removeth stones, may be hurt therewith,—and he that cleaveth wood, may be endangered thereby.
text.KJV: Whoso removeth stones shall be hurt therewith; and he that cleaveth wood shall be endangered thereby.
text.NABRE: Whoever quarries stones may be hurt by them, and whoever chops wood is in danger from it.
text.DRB: He that removeth stones, shall be afflicted in them: and he that cutteth trees, shall be wounded of them.

text.NRSV: If the iron is blunt, and one does not whet the edge, then more strength must be exerted; but wisdom helps one to succeed.
text.Rotherham: If, blunt, be the iron, and, himself, hath not sharpened, the edge, then, much force, must he apply,—but, an advantage for giving success, is wisdom.
text.KJV: If the iron be blunt, and he do not whet the edge, then must he put to more strength: but wisdom is profitable to direct.
text.NABRE: If the ax becomes dull, and the blade is not sharpened, then effort must be increased. But the advantage of wisdom is success.
text.DRB: If the iron shall be blunt, and that not as before, but shall be made blunt, it shall be sharpened by great labour: and after industry shall wisdom follow.

text.NRSV: If the snake bites before it is charmed, there is no advantage in a charmer.
text.Rotherham: If a serpent will bite, unless he is charmed, then there is nothing better for him that owneth a tongue.
text.KJV: Surely the serpent will bite without enchantment; and a babbler is no better.
text.NABRE: If the snake bites before it is charmed, then there is no advantage in a charmer.
text.DRB: If a serpent bite in silence, nothing less than it hath he, that detracteth secretly.

text.NRSV: Words spoken by the wise bring them favor, but the lips of fools consume them.
text.Rotherham: The words of a wise man’s mouth, are pleasant,—but, the lips of a dullard, will swallow him up:
text.KJV: The words of a wise man’s mouth are gracious; but the lips of a fool will swallow up himself.
text.NABRE: Words from the mouth of the wise win favor, but the lips of fools consume them.
text.DRB: The words of the mouth of a wiseman grace: and the lips of the unwise shall throw him down headlong.

text.NRSV: The beginning of their words is folly, and the end of their talk is wicked madness;
text.Rotherham: The beginning of the words of his mouth, is folly,—but, the latter end of his speech, is mischievous madness.
text.KJV: The beginning of the words of his mouth is foolishness: and the end of his talk is mischievous madness.
text.NABRE: The beginning of their words is folly, and the end of their talk is utter madness;
text.DRB: The beginning of his words is folly, and the later end of his mouth is most wicked error.

text.NRSV: yet fools talk on and on. No one knows what is to happen, and who can tell them what will be after them?
text.Rotherham: Yet, a foolish man, multiplieth words,—[though] no man knoweth that which hath been, and, that which shall be after him, who can tell him?
text.KJV: A fool also is full of words: a man cannot tell what shall be; and what shall be after him, who can tell him?
text.NABRE: yet fools multiply words. No one knows what is to come, for who can tell anyone what will be?
text.DRB: Afool multiplieth words. A man is ignorant what hath been before him: and what shall be after him, who can tell him?

text.NRSV: The toil of fools wears them out, for they do not even know the way to town.
text.Rotherham: The toil of dullards, shall weary a man, that he knoweth not how to go into the city.
text.KJV: The labour of the foolish wearieth every one of them, because he knoweth not how to go to the city.
text.NABRE: The toil of fools wearies them, so they do not know even the way to town.
text.DRB: The labour of fools shall afflict them, that know not to go into the city.

text.NRSV: Alas for you, O land, when your king is a lad, and your princes feast in the morning!
text.Rotherham: Alas! for thee, O land, when thy king is a boy,—and, thy rulers, in the morning, do eat:
text.KJV: Woe to thee, O land, when thy king is a child, and thy princes eat in the morning!
text.NABRE: No One Knows What Evil Will Come Woe to you, O land, whose king is a youth, and whose princes feast in the morning!
text.DRB: Woe to thee o land, whose king is a child, and whose Princes eat in the morning.

text.NRSV: Happy are you, O land, when your king is a nobleman, and your princes feast at the proper time—for strength, and not for drunkenness!
text.Rotherham: How happy art thou, O land, when thy king is a son of nobles,—and, thy rulers, in season, do eat, for strength, and not for debauchery.
text.KJV: Blessed art thou, O land, when thy king is the son of nobles, and thy princes eat in due season, for strength, and not for drunkenness!
text.NABRE: Happy are you, O land, whose king is of noble birth, and whose princes dine at the right time— for vigor and not in drinking bouts.
text.DRB: Blessed is the land whose king is noble, & whose Princes eat in their time, to refection, and not to riotousness.

text.NRSV: Through sloth the roof sinks in, and through indolence the house leaks.
text.Rotherham: By two lazy arms, the framework sinketh in,—and, by the hanging down of the hands, the house may leak.
text.KJV: By much slothfulness the building decayeth; and through idleness of the hands the house droppeth through.
text.NABRE: Because of laziness, the rafters sag; when hands are slack, the house leaks.
text.DRB: In slothfulness the roof of the house shall go to ruin, & in the infirmity of the hands the house shall drop through.

text.NRSV: Feasts are made for laughter; wine gladdens life, and money answers everything.
text.Rotherham: Merrily people make bread, and, wine, gladdeneth life,—but, money, answereth all things.
text.KJV: A feast is made for laughter, and wine maketh merry: but money answereth all things.
text.NABRE: A feast is made for merriment and wine gives joy to the living, but money answers for everything.
text.DRB: They make bread for laughter, and wine, that living they may make merry: and to money all things obey.

text.NRSV: Do not curse the king, even in your thoughts, or curse the rich, even in your bedroom; for a bird of the air may carry your voice, or some winged creature tell the matter.
text.Rotherham: Even in thy thought, do not revile, the king, nor, within thy bed-chambers, revile thou the rich,—for, a bird of the heavens, might carry the voice, yea, an owner of wings, might tell the matter.
text.KJV: Curse not the king, no not in thy thought; and curse not the rich in thy bedchamber: for a bird of the air shall carry the voice, and that which hath wings shall tell the matter.
text.NABRE: Even in your thoughts do not curse the king, nor in the privacy of your bedroom curse the rich; For the birds of the air may carry your voice, a winged creature may tell what you say.
text.DRB: In thy cogitation detract not from the king, and in the secret of thy chamber curse not the richman: because even the birds of the air will carry thy voice, and he that hath wings will declare the sentence.

text.NRSV: Send out your bread upon the waters, for after many days you will get it back.
text.Rotherham: Cast thy bread-corn, upon the face of the waters,—for, after many days, shalt thou find it:
text.KJV: Cast thy bread upon the waters: for thou shalt find it after many days.
text.NABRE: Send forth your bread upon the face of the waters; after a long time you may find it again.
text.DRB: Cast thy bread upon the passing waters: because after much time thou shalt find it.

text.NRSV: Divide your means seven ways, or even eight, for you do not know what disaster may happen on earth.
text.Rotherham: Give a portion to seven, yea even to eight,—for thou canst not know, what there shall be of misfortune, upon the earth.
text.KJV: Give a portion to seven, and also to eight; for thou knowest not what evil shall be upon the earth.
text.NABRE: Make seven, or even eight portions; you know not what misfortune may come upon the earth.
text.DRB: Give a portion to seven, and also to eight: because thou knowest not what evil shall be upon the earth:

text.NRSV: When clouds are full, they empty rain on the earth; whether a tree falls to the south or to the north, in the place where the tree falls, there it will lie.
text.Rotherham: If the clouds be filled with a downpour, upon the earth, will they empty themselves, and, if a tree fall in the south or in the north, in the place where the tree falleth, there will it be found.
text.KJV: If the clouds be full of rain, they empty themselves upon the earth: and if the tree fall toward the south, or toward the north, in the place where the tree falleth, there it shall be.
text.NABRE: No One Knows What Good Will Come When the clouds are full, they pour out rain upon the earth. Whether a tree falls to the south or to the north, wherever it falls, there shall it lie.
text.DRB: If the clouds be full, they will pour out rain upon the earth. If the tree shall fall to the South, or the North, in what place soever it shall fall there shall it be.

text.NRSV: Whoever watches the wind will not sow; and whoever looks at the clouds will not reap.
text.Rotherham: He that observeth the wind, will not sow,—and, he that watcheth the clouds, will not reap.
text.KJV: He that observeth the wind shall not sow; and he that regardeth the clouds shall not reap.
text.NABRE: One who pays heed to the wind will never sow, and one who watches the clouds will never reap.
text.DRB: He that observeth the wind, soweth not: and he that considereth the clouds, shall never reap.

text.NRSV: Just as you do not know how the breath comes to the bones in the mother’s womb, so you do not know the work of God, who makes everything.
text.Rotherham: Just as thou knowest not what is the way of the spirit, when the body is in the womb of her that is with child, even, so, canst thou not know the work of God, who maketh all.
text.KJV: As thou knowest not what is the way of the spirit, nor how the bones do grow in the womb of her that is with child: even so thou knowest not the works of God who maketh all.
text.NABRE: Just as you do not know how the life breath enters the human frame in the mother’s womb, So you do not know the work of God, who is working in everything.
text.DRB: As thou art ignorant which is the way of the spirit, & how the bones are framed together in the womb of her that conceiveth child: so thou knowest not the works of God, who is the maker of all.

text.NRSV: In the morning sow your seed, and at evening do not let your hands be idle; for you do not know which will prosper, this or that, or whether both alike will be good.
text.Rotherham: In the morning, sow thy seed, and, until evening, do not withhold thy hand,—for thou knowest not—whether shall thrive, either this or that, or whether, both alike, shall be fruitful.
text.KJV: In the morning sow thy seed, and in the evening withhold not thine hand: for thou knowest not whether shall prosper, either this or that, or whether they both shall be alike good.
text.NABRE: In the morning sow your seed, and at evening do not let your hand be idle: For you do not know which of the two will be successful, or whether both alike will turn out well.
text.DRB: In the morning sow thy seed, and in the evening let not thy hand cease: for thou knowest not which may rather spring, this or that: and if both together, it shall be the better.

text.NRSV: Light is sweet, and it is pleasant for the eyes to see the sun.
text.Rotherham: Truly sweet is the light,—and, pleasant to the eyes, to see the sun:
text.KJV: Truly the light is sweet, and a pleasant thing it is for the eyes to behold the sun:
text.NABRE: Poem on Youth and Old Age. Light is sweet! and it is pleasant for the eyes to see the sun.
text.DRB: The light is sweet: and it is delectable for the eyes to see the sun.

text.NRSV: Even those who live many years should rejoice in them all; yet let them remember that the days of darkness will be many. All that comes is vanity.
text.Rotherham: But, though, many years, a man live, through them all, let him rejoice; yet let him remember the days of darkness, for many they may be, all that cometh, may be vanity.
text.KJV: But if a man live many years, and rejoice in them all; yet let him remember the days of darkness; for they shall be many. All that cometh is vanity.
text.NABRE: However many years mortals may live, let them, as they enjoy them all, remember that the days of darkness will be many. All that is to come is vanity.
text.DRB: If a man shall live many years, and shall have rejoiced in them all, he must remember the darksome time, and many days, which when they shall come, the things past shall be reproved of vanity.

text.NRSV: Rejoice, young man, while you are young, and let your heart cheer you in the days of your youth. Follow the inclination of your heart and the desire of your eyes, but know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment.
text.Rotherham: Rejoice, O young man, in thy youth, and let thy heart gladden thee in the days of thine early manhood, and walk thou—in the ways of thine own heart, and in that which is seen by thine own eyes,—yet know, that, for all these things, will God bring thee into judgment.
text.KJV: Rejoice, O young man, in thy youth; and let thy heart cheer thee in the days of thy youth, and walk in the ways of thine heart, and in the sight of thine eyes: but know thou, that for all these things God will bring thee into judgment.
text.NABRE: Rejoice, O youth, while you are young and let your heart be glad in the days of your youth. Follow the ways of your heart, the vision of your eyes; Yet understand regarding all this that God will bring you to judgment.
text.DRB: Rejoice therefore youngman in thy youth, and let thy heart be in good, in the days of thy youth, and walk in the ways of thy heart, and in the sight of thine eyes: and know that for all these God will bring thee into judgement.

text.NRSV: Banish anxiety from your mind, and put away pain from your body; for youth and the dawn of life are vanity.
text.Rotherham: Therefore remove thou vexation from thy heart, and put away discomfort from thy flesh,—for, youth and dawn, are vanity!
text.KJV: Therefore remove sorrow from thy heart, and put away evil from thy flesh: for childhood and youth are vanity.
text.NABRE: Banish misery from your heart and remove pain from your body, for youth and black hair are fleeting.
text.DRB: Take away anger from thy heart, and remove malice from thy flesh. For youth and pleasure are vain.

text.NRSV: Remember your creator in the days of your youth, before the days of trouble come, and the years draw near when you will say, “I have no pleasure in them”;
text.Rotherham: Yet remember thy Creator, in the days of thy vigour,—or ever come in, the days of discomfort, and the years arrive, in which thou shalt say—I have, in them, no pleasure;
text.KJV: Remember now thy Creator in the days of thy youth, while the evil days come not, nor the years draw nigh, when thou shalt say, I have no pleasure in them;
text.NABRE: Remember your Creator in the days of your youth, before the evil days come And the years approach of which you will say, “I have no pleasure in them”;
text.DRB: Remember thy creator in the days of thy youth, before the time of affliction come, & the years approach, of which thou mayst say: They please me not:

text.NRSV: before the sun and the light and the moon and the stars are darkened and the clouds return with the rain;
text.Rotherham: Or ever be darkened—the sun, and the light, and the moon, and the stars,—and the clouds return after a downpour of rain;
text.KJV: While the sun, or the light, or the moon, or the stars, be not darkened, nor the clouds return after the rain:
text.NABRE: Before the sun is darkened and the light and the moon and the stars and the clouds return after the rain;
text.DRB: before the sun, and light, and moon, and stars be dark, and the clouds return after the rain:

text.NRSV: in the day when the keepers of the house tremble, and the strong men are bent, and the women who grind cease because they are few, and those who look through the windows see dimly;
text.Rotherham: In the day when the keepers of the house shall tremble, and the men of might bow themselves,—and the grinders cease because they are few, and they who look through the windows are darkened;
text.KJV: In the day when the keepers of the house shall tremble, and the strong men shall bow themselves, and the grinders cease because they are few, and those that look out of the windows be darkened,
text.NABRE: When the guardians of the house tremble, and the strong men are bent; When the women who grind are idle because they are few, and those who look through the windows grow blind;
text.DRB: when the keepers of the house shall be moved, and the strongest men shall stagger, and the grinders shall be idle in a small number: and they shall wax dark that look through the holes:

text.NRSV: when the doors on the street are shut, and the sound of the grinding is low, and one rises up at the voice of a bird, and all the daughters of song are brought low;
text.Rotherham: And the doors in the street be closed, when the sound of the mill become low,—and one rise at the chirp of a small bird, and low-voiced be all the daughters of song;
text.KJV: And the doors shall be shut in the streets, when the sound of the grinding is low, and he shall rise up at the voice of the bird, and all the daughters of musick shall be brought low;
text.NABRE: When the doors to the street are shut, and the sound of the mill is low; When one rises at the call of a bird, and all the daughters of song are quiet;
text.DRB: and they shall shut the doors in the street, at the baseness of the grinder's voice, and they shall rise up at the voice of the bird, and all the daughters of song shall be deaf.

text.NRSV: when one is afraid of heights, and terrors are in the road; the almond tree blossoms, the grasshopper drags itself along and desire fails; because all must go to their eternal home, and the mourners go about the streets;
text.Rotherham: Yea, at what is high, they be in fear, and there be, terrors, in the way, and the almond be rejected, and the grasshopper drag itself along, and desire perish,—for man is going to his age-abiding home, when the wailers shall go round in the streets;
text.KJV: Also when they shall be afraid of that which is high, and fears shall be in the way, and the almond tree shall flourish, and the grasshopper shall be a burden, and desire shall fail: because man goeth to his long home, and the mourners go about the streets:
text.NABRE: When one is afraid of heights, and perils in the street; When the almond tree blooms, and the locust grows sluggish and the caper berry is without effect, Because mortals go to their lasting home, and mourners go about the streets;
text.DRB: The high things also shall fear, and they shall be afraid in the way, the almond tree shall flourish, the locust shall be fatted, and the caper tree shall be destroyed: because man shall go into the house of his eternity, and the mourners shall go round about in the street.

text.NRSV: before the silver cord is snapped, and the golden bowl is broken, and the pitcher is broken at the fountain, and the wheel broken at the cistern,
text.Rotherham: Or ever the silver cord be loosed, or the golden bowl be broken,—or the bucket by the fountain be shivered, or the wheel at the well be broken;
text.KJV: Or ever the silver cord be loosed, or the golden bowl be broken, or the pitcher be broken at the fountain, or the wheel broken at the cistern.
text.NABRE: Before the silver cord is snapped and the golden bowl is broken, And the pitcher is shattered at the spring, and the pulley is broken at the well,
text.DRB: Before the silver cord be broken, and the golden head-band recur, and the water-pot be broken upon the fountain, and the wheel be broken upon the cistern,

text.NRSV: and the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the breath returns to God who gave it.
text.Rotherham: And the dust return to the earth, as it was,—and, the spirit, return unto God, who gave it.
text.KJV: Then shall the dust return to the earth as it was: and the spirit shall return unto God who gave it.
text.NABRE: And the dust returns to the earth as it once was, and the life breath returns to God who gave it.
text.DRB: and the dust return into his earth, from whence it was, and the spirit return to God, who gave it.

text.NRSV: Vanity of vanities, says the Teacher; all is vanity.
text.Rotherham: Vanity of vanities, saith the Proclaimer, all, is vanity.
text.KJV: Vanity of vanities, saith the preacher; all is vanity.
text.NABRE: Vanity of vanities, says Qoheleth, all things are vanity!
text.DRB: Vanity of vanities, said Ecclesiastes, and all things vanity.

text.NRSV: Besides being wise, the Teacher also taught the people knowledge, weighing and studying and arranging many proverbs.
text.Rotherham: Besides that, the Proclaimer being wise,—still further taught knowledge unto the people, and weighed and searched, arranged proverbs in abundance.
text.KJV: And moreover, because the preacher was wise, he still taught the people knowledge; yea, he gave good heed, and sought out, and set in order many proverbs.
text.NABRE: Epilogue. Besides being wise, Qoheleth taught the people knowledge, and weighed, scrutinized and arranged many proverbs.
text.DRB: And whereas Ecclesiastes was most wise, he taught the people, and declared the things that he had done: and searching forth made many parables.

text.NRSV: The Teacher sought to find pleasing words, and he wrote words of truth plainly.
text.Rotherham: The Proclaimer sought to find out words giving delight, and to note down rightly, the words of truth.
text.KJV: The preacher sought to find out acceptable words: and that which was written was upright, even words of truth.
text.NABRE: Qoheleth sought to find appropriate sayings, and to write down true sayings with precision.
text.DRB: He sought profitable words, and wrote words most right, and full of truth.

text.NRSV: The sayings of the wise are like goads, and like nails firmly fixed are the collected sayings that are given by one shepherd.
text.Rotherham: The words of the wise, are as goads, yea, as driven nails, their well-ordered sayings,—given from one shepherd.
text.KJV: The words of the wise are as goads, and as nails fastened by the masters of assemblies, which are given from one shepherd.
text.NABRE: The sayings of the wise are like goads; like fixed spikes are the collected sayings given by one shepherd.
text.DRB: The words of wisemen are as pricks, and as nails deeply strucken in, which by the counsel of masters are given of one pastor.

text.NRSV: Of anything beyond these, my child, beware. Of making many books there is no end, and much study is a weariness of the flesh.
text.Rotherham: And besides, from them, my son, be admonished,—Of making many books, there is no end, and, much study, is a weariness of the flesh.
text.KJV: And further, by these, my son, be admonished: of making many books there is no end; and much study is a weariness of the flesh.
text.NABRE: As to more than these, my son, beware. Of the making of many books there is no end, and in much study there is weariness for the flesh.
text.DRB: More than these my son require not. Of making many books there is no end: and often meditation is affliction of the flesh.

text.NRSV: The end of the matter; all has been heard. Fear God, and keep his commandments; for this is the whole duty of everyone.
text.Rotherham: The conclusion of the matter—the whole, let us hear,—Towards God, be reverent, and, his commandments, observe, for, this, [concerneth] all mankind.
text.KJV: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man.
text.NABRE: The last word, when all is heard: Fear God and keep his commandments, for this concerns all humankind;
text.DRB: Let us all hear together the end of speaking. Fear God, and observe his commandments: for this is every man:

text.NRSV: For God will bring every deed into judgment, including every secret thing, whether good or evil.
text.Rotherham: For, every work, will God bring into judgment, with every hidden thing,—whether good, or evil.
text.KJV: For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil.
text.NABRE: because God will bring to judgment every work, with all its hidden qualities, whether good or bad.
text.DRB: and all things that are done, God will bring into judgement for every error, whether it be good or evil.

5.20: For they will hardly dwell on the shortness of life, because God lets them busy themselves with the joy of their heart.
